text: This probably isn't the first time you've heard that Large Language Models (LLMs) and generative AI have fundamentally changed what it means to build software. We've moved from telling computers exactly what to do, step by step, to showing them examples and watching them run with it. Advances in model efficiency, open-source tools like ollama that run on our own machines, and highly compressed (quantized) models have together democratized generative AI in a way that would have seemed impossible just a few years ago. GenAI is no longer a luxury reserved for big tech companies with massive GPU clusters: it's a programmable resource within reach of every student, academic, and curious tinkerer who wants to experiment. This book is an example-driven journey through practical AI-assisted software engineering. Together we'll explore the core concepts you'll need to succeed in modern software development, demonstrated with freely available tools and models. Every demo and experiment comes with illustrations and explanations, and the code is included so you can reproduce each one yourself. Every concept comes with carefully crafted Python examples that you can run, modify, learn from, and reuse to kickstart your own projects. I've also included a few of my best attempts at humor, and I've tried not to lean too hard on Gen Z slang and memes, though I can't speak for gemma4:

Later in this book we'll build Sophia, an AI assistant whose whole job is to be knowledgable about this book. When the manuscript was nearly finished, I asked for a poem about the book, and here it is as a bit of an introduction: Building brains, token by next guess.\ Crafting whispers with careful dials.\ Invisible slices, the model's breath.\ Meaning mapped onto colored space.\ Code appearing from pure language.\ Tethering knowledge to retrieved facts.\ Hands extending: tools and recall.\ Watching the agent walk through the world.\ Thought unfolding, layer by layer thought.\ Pixels bloom, then sound echoes forth.\ Guardrails built against the unseen fray.\ Shrinking giants to fit the small core.\ — Sophia (sometimes powered by gemma4), asked to summarize the book Programming Generative AI

This book was built on Python 3.13.7, and it uses Ollama and its Python API heavily. Ollama is a free and open-source tool that makes running LLMs locally simple. Everything executes on your own machine, and you choose which models to pull, how to configure them, and when to run them. That control is what makes Ollama so valuable in educational settings, where transparency, reproducibility, and hands-on experimentation are essential. With Ollama, students can work with modern open-weight models, including general-purpose language models, code-focused models, specialized models such as medical ones, and embedding models, without relying on external APIs or an internet connection. Experimentation costs nothing, privacy is preserved, and students can observe system behavior directly, including token usage, context limits, and output variability. In the college-level courses where this book is currently used, Ollama provides a laboratory environment for studying generative AI as a transparent system rather than a black box. Students explore core concepts such as prompt structure, sampling parameters, model tradeoffs, and retrieval-augmented generation as they work through guided labs. When they run models locally and embed them into their own applications, students begin to build practical intuition for how large language models work and how they fail, where their limits lie, and how to use them responsibly and effectively in real production systems.
heading: A Few Notes on Book Software

text: Several of the illustrations in this book are generated by FLUX.1-schnell, an open-source image-generation model released by Black Forest Labs . It's used here under the Apache 2.0 license, which permits use, reproduction, and distribution with attribution. A few illustrations come from earlier open models, and each is credited the same way. Every one of these images is labeled as AI-generated in its caption, with the exact prompt printed alongside it. Readers can see what instruction produced each image, and are free to experiment with the same prompts themselves using this open model. Several of the prompts recreate demonstrations that became landmarks in the field, like DALL-E's avocado armchair, and those captions also point back to the original research work.
heading: A Few Notes on Book Software

text: Throughout this book we'll use the most popular open-source models available at the time of writing. No single model suits every purpose, so we'll rely primarily on eight core models that are either strong all-rounders or specialists in a particular craft: coding, vision, creative writing, embeddings, and so on. A lot of modern software engineering comes down to having the experience and judgment to pick the right model for the right job. Reading through this book, you might think that I just knew which models would suit which demos and illustrations, but these choices came after hours of trial and error. If you find yourself struggling with a model on a particular task, thinking something like "Why can't this model understand this? It's so easy! Argh!", don't fight it too hard; try a few different models on the same task. The difference might surprise you. This is a recurring theme in this book.
heading: Our Co-Instructors

text: All of these models will prove useful at various points in our exploration of generative AI. A few more contestants, mistral, ministral-3, qwen3.5, and the tiny functiongemma, join later, when the Agentic, Autonomy, and Responsible chapters hold their auditions and let measurements pick the winners.
heading: Our Co-Instructors

text: Abstract This chapter explains the philosophy behind the whole book: the best way to learn how generative AI works is to run it yourself, on your own laptop, rather than read about someone else's demo. We describe our audience: anyone curious enough to follow along, with no math required and only a little Python. Then we look at what the word 'generative' really means, how it differs from the discriminative AI that just sorts things into boxes. By the end we'll see that a language model is at heart a prediction machine: it completes patterns it picked up during training and forecasts the next word as a probability distribution, which is also where its confident mistakes come from.

The insights in this book draw on several parts of my education, most of all my time teaching at Walla Walla University, where I designed and ran hands-on labs with students in several classes like Software Engineering, Artificial Intelligence, and my favorite Introduction to Generative AI. When we started weaving generative AI into those courses, it went better than I'd hoped. Students with no experience developing with AI were suddenly learning and building amazing things that surprised me. I'm grateful to my students for that energy and curiosity. It was quite a challenge designing so many new generative AI courses during my initial few years of full-time teaching. I wanted my pain to mean something, so I wrote a book about my experience (this one). I hope other people getting into generative AI will find it a useful resource. My research background spans a few subfields: system verification, machine learning, and privacy-preserving technology, all right at the intersection of data science and formal reasoning. I've also worked on programming language design (we all love coding, right?). I think my security-minded background helps me approach AI with some healthy skepticism. I'm enthusiastic about what these systems can do, but I'm also careful to ask: what are the guarantees? What could go wrong?
heading: Author's Note

text: The proliferation of generative AI has made conversations about testing, verification, and guardrails in software more important than ever. Some people have drifted away from computer science because it feels like the machines can do it all now. But I say this is one of the most exciting times to be a programmer, we can express our creativity more freely than ever before. It still takes real skill and discipline to build something noteworthy. On its own, a language model seems unlikely to create anything truly original, either helpful or harmful. But with you guiding it, teaching it, and applying sound judgment? That's where things could get very, very interesting. As AI automates the how, the builder's value is shifting toward the what and the why. The engineer of the future looks more like a sci-fi system architect calmly dictating plans to an attentive robot, and less like someone staring at Stack Overflow all day. Stack Overflow's own traffic started sliding as developers made that switch .
heading: Author's Note

text: You can think of this as a guidebook for the lab portion of your generative AI classes, or, if your class is more hands-on, as the main textbook. All the code to reproduce the demos and experiments is included, and you can use it to bootstrap your lab design. I don't go very deep into theory here; this is more of a builder's or software-engineering book than a machine-learning one. Several other books cover the theoretical side well. It's suited to a 100- or 200-level technology-oriented class with no prerequisites. Yes, it's a book for everyone: for the economists, the marine biologists, and everyone in between. Enjoy!
heading: For Educators

text: This material is written especially for students building mental models of AI tools, teachers who want to bring AI into their curricula, and practitioners integrating GenAI into their workflows. The goal is to make AI concrete and comprehensible, so you can use it productively and know when to trust it and when to lean on your own judgment instead. This book is written for people who are curious about generative AI and want to understand it by seeing it in action and building with it, not just by reading about it. It's for people who want to have some fun while they learn, and to put these tools to work in their own lives and jobs in a measured, thoughtful way. You won't need a math background to follow the prose, or an advanced programming background to read the book. Some basic familiarity with Python will help you get more out of the code sections, but even if this is your first time seeing code, the examples are designed to read as close to plain English as possible. If you find yourself unsure about a line of code, and you have ollama installed (which I recommend), you can always ask qwen2.5-coder or your favorite open-weights coding model to explain it to you. This book suits undergraduate students, professionals who want to understand how to use generative AI well, and curious readers who want a closer look at the mechanics of AI, explained in plain language.
heading: Audience

text: You don't have to install anything to read this book. Every code example prints its output right there on the page, so you can follow each demo and experiment from start to finish without running a single cell. The numbers, transcripts, and figures you'll see are saved snapshots of real runs. Setting up to run the code yourself is optional, and worth doing if you want to reproduce the experiments, change a prompt and watch the answer shift, or build a lab around these chapters for a class. All of the code runs locally on our personal computers. We use Ollama to run open-source (or open-weights) language models on your machine, and a small companion library called genai that keeps the code shown in the book concise and neat. You can go ahead and install Ollama and pull the models we use with something like ollama pull gemma4, install uv and create a project environment with uv sync, then open any chapter notebook in Jupyter and run the cells top to bottom. The genai library, lives in the companion GitHub repository.
heading: What We'll Need

text: Programming Generative AI is executable! Each chapter in this book is a self-contained Jupyter Notebook. Every concept comes with code examples alongside the prose. The examples are intentionally short and direct. A lot of implementation detail lives in the genai library. The goal is that the code in the chapter itself reads like a clean illustration of the idea, not a production ready system. The outputs printed in the book are saved snapshots of real runs. I show all the hits and misses because a recorded failure can teach more than a perfect demo. The companion repository includes the scripts that reproduce every demo and experiment. A consequence of working with generative models is that your numbers won't exactly match mine. Ask the same model the same question twice and you can get two different answers; that isn't a bug, and the Prompting chapter explains exactly where the randomness comes from. What should survive the stochasticity is the shape of each result: which techniques can help, which model holds up, which failure keeps happening. While certain things may appear random in one run, there is usually a coherent pattern over many.
heading: Design Philosophy

text: 1. Run the setup check from earlier in this chapter, print(ask("What does generative mean?")), and read the answer your model gives back. Then run the very same line two or three more times. The wording will drift a little on each run, and that small bit of wandering is your first real glimpse of how these models work. We'll explain exactly why it happens in the chapters ahead. 2. Pull a second model with ollama pull (try a small one like gemma3:1b alongside a larger one like gemma4), then ask both the same question and compare the replies. Notice what you gain and what you give up as the model gets bigger.
heading: Warm-Up

text: Most of the AI you've probably seen in real life, in what now feels like the distant past (~1945-2020), is what we call discriminative AI. For example, a spam filter might look at an overly lengthy chat message and decide: spam or not spam. A recommendation system watches your viewing history and predicts: you'll probably like this movie next. A chess-playing agent looks at the game state and decides the next best move. In all of these cases, the AI is mainly choosing between existing options. It's discriminating between categories, labels, or actions. The system isn't creating something fundamentally new; it's analyzing existing data to categorize it, predict outcomes, or make decisions. Generative AI works very differently. Instead of only classifying or predicting, a generative model learns the underlying patterns and structure of data well enough to generate entirely new content. When you type a prompt into a chatbot, the model generates a response one token at a time, continually predicting what comes next based on everything it's seen before. That's why these systems are capable of such a wide array of very general tasks: writing essays, code, summaries, even creating images, and music. They can simulate conversation.
heading: Consider the LEGO

text: In 1950, when the first general-purpose electronic computers were only about five years old , fluent human-like conversation seemed almost impossible for machines. This led pioneering AI scientist Alan Turing to propose the Turing Test in his paper "Computing Machinery and Intelligence." If a human judge can't reliably differentiate between a machine and another human through text communication alone, then the machine could be considered "intelligent" . Today, such communication is one of the most popular capabilities of AI systems, although most chatbots you'll encounter won't be trying very hard to trick you into thinking they are human. It's like the difference between a jigsaw puzzle and a box of LEGOs. Stay with me: with a jigsaw puzzle, the picture on the lid is already decided, every piece has exactly one correct place, and our job is to figure out where each piece goes. Discriminative AI. Now let's consider the LEGOs. We have a pile of bricks and you can build a castle, a huge panda, or something nobody has ever built before. The pieces are familiar, but what you make from them could be new, depending on how much of your own creative influence you add. That's generative AI.
heading: Consider the LEGO

text: A jigsaw puzzle has one right answer decided before you begin; a box of LEGOs can become a castle, a spaceship, or something nobody has built before. Discriminative AI solves the puzzle, generative AI plays with the bricks. Both images generated by AI (FLUX.1-schnell ).
heading: Consider the LEGO

text: Let's look at a discriminative example below. It analyzes a piece of text that already exists, here a short movie review by yours truly, and the model's job is to decide which label fits. This is a tiny jigsaw puzzle, where a piece is given and there are only a few slots it could belong in.
heading: Consider the LEGO

text: Modern LLMs can actually do both discriminative and generative tasks. That doesn't mean they're equally good at both. A classical AI algorithm that's explicitly programmed with heuristics and maximizes a utility function, or even a plain Monte Carlo simulation, will still outperform an LLM at a tightly defined game, like chess or checkers. To see what that looks like, let's hand one tricky chess position to two different sizes of our LLM, and then bring in a real chess engine for comparison. The position comes from a well-known opening trap: Black has just thrown a knight out to a5 to attack White's bishop, and White needs a reply that gets the bishop to safety. None of these LLMs were ever explicitly taught chess. They only absorbed whatever games and commentary happened to drift through their training data, so what we're watching is pattern-matching, not calculation. The prompt itself spells the position out as a list of moves in standard chess notation and as a FEN string, which is just a compact one-line encoding of exactly where every piece sits.
heading: Consider the LEGO

text: Three players, three different answers, and the larger LLM comes off worst. gemma4:latest plays Bxa5 and defends it with total confidence, telling us it "captures the attacking knight." Just one problem: that move is illegal. The bishop sitting on c4 is a light-squared bishop, and a5 is a dark square, so the bishop simply can't travel there, capture or no capture. The model has produced a fluent, authoritative sentence about a move that doesn't exist on this board. The smaller gemma4:e2b does better in a narrow sense. It plays Bb3, a perfectly legal retreat that tucks the bishop safely out of the knight's reach. It's a bit timid, but at least it's a move we're allowed to make. A real chess engine, Stockfish, plays Bb5+. It runs a structured search and suggests Bb5+ a reply that's both legal and delivers check. Saving the bishop while also checking the king forces Black to respond and hands White a free move. The bigger model stumbled because it was never really reasoning about a board in the first place. It was assembling plausible, chess-flavored text, and "capture the piece that is attacking you" is an extremely plausible sentence that just happens to break the rules here. This is our first hallucination example.
heading: Consider the LEGO

text: At a high level, a generative model does something simple to describe, even if the engineering underneath is anything but: it learns what things tend to look like, and then it produces new things that look like that. It learns which LEGO bricks tend to fit next to which others, and then it builds. The technical term for what the model is learning is a probability distribution over the data. Which just means the model is learning which answers are more likely and which aren't, based on everything it's seen.
heading: How GenAI Works

text: That answer felt obvious, but why was the model so sure? We can check our receipts here by pulling up the model's forecast for the next word. Here we let llama3.2 finish the phrase directly and peek at the probabilities it assigns to the next token.
heading: How GenAI Works

text: The distribution is steeply peaked. The model pours almost all of its confidence, about 95%, onto "Washington" and scatters only tiny slivers across filler like an ellipsis, an article, or a fragment of the word itself. No other city shows up at all. The model has seen that sentence completed enough times that the answer is barely in question. Not every prompt is this lopsided, though. Watch what happens when we hand the model the opening of a story instead of a fact, so the next word is up for grabs.
heading: How GenAI Works

text: This time, no single word owns the distribution. The model's favorite, "man," gets only about 18%, and a whole crowd of equally reasonable continuations lines up right behind it: a small something, a figure, a group, a beautiful something. The probability is spread, because a door can open onto almost anything. But watch the second panel. The moment the model commits to its favorite, "man," it forecasts all over again, and this time the field collapses: about 71% of its confidence pours onto "standing." A door can open onto anything, but once a person is standing in it, the sentence almost finishes itself. That is the engine, rolling the dice one word at a time, each choice reshaping the odds for the one that follows. The peaks gives us reliability, the spread gives us creativity, and learning to sense which one we're standing in is a real part of working with these systems. But there's a step we skipped over. To forecast the next word at all, the model first has to decide which of the words already in front of it matter most.
heading: How GenAI Works

text: Look again at "She opened the door and saw a man... standing." When the model reached for that second word, it didn't weigh every earlier word equally. "Standing" follows from "man," not from "door" or "saw." Somewhere inside, the model had to look back across the sentence and decide that "man" was the word that mattered most for what came next. That mechanic has a name: attention. For each word it processes, the model sends out a small query, roughly "what should I be paying attention to right now?" Every other word answers with a key, a short label advertising what it offers, and the words whose keys best match the query get pulled in, each handing over its value, the information it carries. This is just like the way our own eyes flick back to an earlier noun the moment you hit the word "it." We can read this BERT, a bidirectional encoder model, which just means it can reads the whole sentence at once, which we need to show attention below. Here we hand it one sentence with the pronoun "it" and watch which earlier word it ties that "it" to, then swap a single important word and watch the semantic patterns change.
heading: What the Model Pays Attention To

text: Attention from the word "it" to each earlier word, read from one head of a language model (BERT ). Swap a single crucial word and the link flips: a tired "it" points back to the cat, a warm "it" points to the laptop.
heading: What the Model Pays Attention To

text: In the first sentence almost all of "it"'s attention lands on "cat": the model has tied the pronoun to the cat, which is the thing that can be tired. Change one word, "tired" to "warm," and the very same "it" swings over to "laptop," the thing that runs warm and that a cat would happily sit on. Just a single word changed, and the model rewires what "it" refers to. This is just one attention head; a real model runs dozens of them side by side across many layers, each free to specialize in a different kind of relationship, and eventually combining the results .
heading: What the Model Pays Attention To

text: Next we put the pattern-completion engine to work. If the model is always continuing the most plausible text, weighing the words already in front of it to decide what comes next, then the words we hand it at the start are the biggest lever we have. Learning to pull that lever on purpose, and to set the dials that tilt the model toward reliability or creativity, is what Prompting is all about.
heading: Conclusion

text: 1. Sort the following everyday tasks into the two buckets from this chapter, jigsaw (discriminative) or LEGO (generative): flagging an email as spam, writing a birthday poem for a friend, deciding whether a photo contains a cat, and naming a brand-new product. For any that feel like they could go in either bucket, write a sentence on why. 2. Use nexttokendistribution and plotnexttoken, the same tools we used for "The capital of the USA is", on two prompts of your own: one where you expect the model to be very sure of the next word, and one where almost anything could come next. Predict which will be steeply peaked and which will be spread out before you run them, then see if you were right.
heading: Exercises

text: Abstract Writing the prompt is the single most important skill for working with a language model. The way you phrase a request shapes what you get back. We start at the control panel, the sampling parameters like temperature, top-k, and top-p that decide whether a model plays it safe or gets creative. We learn to steer with examples through zero-shot, one-shot, and few-shot prompting. Then we climb a short ladder of reasoning techniques, from chain-of-thought to step-back, while staying alert to a model's knack for sounding completely confident while being completely wrong.

Ask a model to vibe-code the video game Snake in Pygame. Intro CS courses have leaned on Pygame for years, and students have been posting their versions to GitHub and GitLab the entire time, so several LLMs were trained on very many Snake programs. So our LLM never struggles to write Snake, but which Snake did you mean? Ask for the game with no other detail and you get the single most common version of it, a hundred small choices already made for you. Getting a Snake that's yours is the harder trick, and it opens a small window into how prompting works.
heading: Snakes in the Bytes

text: I've come to appreciate more and more that the way you ask matters as much as what you ask: two people can put the same underlying question to the same model and get very different answers depending on how they frame it. This is the domain known as prompt engineering: the craft of designing inputs that guide a language model toward exactly the output you need. A language model is a prediction machine, trained on enormous amounts of text to guess what's most likely to come next. So a prompt isn't just a question. It's the beginning of a pattern, and the model's job is to produce a plausible continuation.
heading: Snakes in the Bytes

text: Notice how each prompt sets the model up differently. The first is neutral. The second frames the response as expert opinion. The third implies urgency and authority. The model responds to these cues because its training has taught it which kinds of text tend to follow which kinds of openings. This is your first lesson in prompt engineering: the prompt is context. You aren't asking a question so much as you're establishing a pattern for the model to complete.
heading: Snakes in the Bytes

text: So a build is labelled walls: WRAP if either: 1) The code uses modulo arithmetic against a grid dimension. That modulo is the telltale sign that the head teleports to the opposite edge instead of dying. 2) The literal word "wrap" appears anywhere in the source. Otherwise it falls through to GAME OVER, the classic Snake where touching an edge ends the run. When I just asked for the default Snake the model reached for the version it's seen ten thousand times, walls and all. Touch an edge and the run is over: In the version with a twist our Snake also twists: instead of ending the run, the head now slides off one side and reappears on the other: The skill on display here isn't getting the model to write code. It's noticing all the small decisions it'll otherwise make for you, and naming the few you care about. However, if we push this too far, piling on a stack of unusual rules and instructions at once, we will see the flip side: the model starts dropping the requests that fight hardest against the version it knows best, snapping back toward the familiar. Sometimes it actually does not take very much to trigger this pushback, it could happen on the very first instruction. The gravitational pull, toward what it's seen most and knows best, has a name: misguided attention, and we'll take a look at that later in this chapter.
heading: Snakes in the Bytes

text: Before we go deeper on prompts themselves, we need to understand the settings that control how a model generates text. Back in the Introduction we watched the model lay out its forecast for the next word; the settings on this panel decide how it draws from that forecast. Temperature controls how willing the model is to pick less likely words. At temperature 0, it always picks the single most probable next word, a strategy researchers call greedy decoding because the model just takes the highest-probability token at every step and commits to it. At temperature 0 the model is completely predictable and deterministic. At higher temperatures, it starts considering less likely options, which produces more varied and creative output. Too high and it really becomes AI slop, we'll see some examples of that later on.
heading: The Control Panel: Model Parameters

text: If you run this a few times, the pattern becomes clear. At temperature 0 the opening is locked in. At 0.7 we land in the same dusty attic with the same single shaft of light, just a phantom traded for a ghost and a faded portrait for a tarnished locket. By 1.5 the model is taking real risks, wandering off toward old libraries and distant sea brine.
heading: The Control Panel: Model Parameters

text: OpenAI's Codex team measured this directly, and the best temperature turned out to depend entirely on how many attempts you planned to make . When they kept a single completion, accuracy peaked at a low temperature, around 0.2. When they instead sampled a hundred candidates and kept the best one, the sweet spot jumped to around 0.8, because at that point you want the pile to be diverse and you only need one of them to be good. Later program-synthesis work found the same shape , and it's the very logic behind self-consistency, which we'll also discuss more later in the Thinking chapter, where we deliberately sample several different reasoning paths and let our models vote on the answer .
heading: The Control Panel: Model Parameters

text: Temperature changes how the model weighs its options. Top-K and Top-P change what options it considers in the first place. Top-K limits the model to the K most likely next tokens. Set K to 10 and the model only ever picks from the top 10 candidates at each step, no matter how confident or uncertain it is.
heading: The Control Panel: Model Parameters

text: Notice how little changes between the two runs. That similarity is the lesson: when the model is confident about a common question, the top five candidates already carry most of the weight, so opening the gate to fifty barely lets anything new through. Top-K matters on the uncertain steps, not the obvious ones.
heading: The Control Panel: Model Parameters

text: Top-P (nucleus sampling) is a bit smarter about this. Instead of a fixed count, it picks from the smallest group of tokens whose combined probability adds up to at least P. The "nucleus" part of the name just means the dense core of likely candidates, the words that together account for most of the probability mass. Everything outside the nucleus is trimmed. As we saw in the the Introduction chapter When the model is very confident, like when completing "The capital of France is...", the top few tokens account for almost all of the probability mass. When the model is uncertain, probability spreads out across many tokens, and Top-P expands the pool accordingly. Let's take a visual look at how these controls affect probability and choice, going off our running examples from last chapter.
heading: The Control Panel: Model Parameters

text: Both panels show the very same five guesses, the model's real probabilities for what comes after that door. On the left, temperature never changes our options, only how differently the model chooses among them: cool it to 0.5 and the front-runner man pulls away, heat it up to 1.5 and the whole field levels out so group and beautiful might get a real say. On the right, top-k and top-p instead decide who is allowed in the room at all. Top-k keeps a fixed headcount, the three tallest, and draws its orange line in the same place no matter what. Top-p keeps the smallest set of words whose probabilities clear 0.9, which here means four words (highlighted in blue). That's the split in one picture: temperature changes how the model picks, while top-k and top-p change what it gets to pick from.
heading: The Control Panel: Model Parameters

text: This time the two openings really do diverge. At topp=0.2 the model is boxed into the densest, most expected tokens, so it hands us the stock noir cliché: rain-slicked cobblestones under a flickering gaslight. At topp=1.0 nothing gets filtered, and the model reaches past the obvious into the tail of the distribution for something stranger, an antique locket humming with silence. A low ceiling keeps the model on the safe, well-worn path, and a high one lets it consider the rarer words where the more surprising writing tends to live.
heading: The Control Panel: Model Parameters

text: Most local runtimes apply them in this order: Top-K filters to K candidates, Top-P further narrows based on cumulative probability, and temperature then reshapes whatever remains before the model draws from it. Some frameworks run temperature first instead, but the intuition holds either way: the filters decide which words are even on the table, and temperature decides how the model picks among them.
heading: The Control Panel: Model Parameters

text: The first row is the model on its best behavior. The second leaves topk and topp at their usual values but pushes the temperature to 2.0, and the sentence still holds together, just a touch more lyrical. The third row lifts both filters (topk=0 lets every token compete, topp=1.0 trims nothing) and keeps the temperature at 2.0. The result is complete wreckage: sentences that say nothing, with stray vocabulary from the model's own world ("computational", "generative", "algorithmically") surfacing in what should have been a sunny beach scene. That gap between the second and third rows is the lesson. Temperature is the accelerator, and Top-K and Top-P are the brakes. A high temperature on its own rarely produces gibberish, because the two filters are still discarding the least likely tokens before the model rolls its weighted die. Lift the filters and max out the temperature at the same time, and the long tail of barely-plausible tokens stampede in. This is the failure mode behind the earlier warning about temperature 2.0, and it's why the practical ranges stopped at 1.5.
heading: The Control Panel: Model Parameters

text: Sampling controls govern how the model says things; the prompt itself governs what you're asking for. And a surprising share of bad answers are less the model's fault than a sign that the prompt left too much unsaid, so it helps to think of a prompt as having a few moving parts you can imrpove one at a time. There's the instruction, the thing you want done, which lands best when it's specific and phrased as a direct request rather than a vague wish. There's the context the model needs but which is like the backstory for the instructin. Then there's the output format, where a line like "answer with a single word" or "return JSON with these keys" saves you from parsing a friendly paragraph you never asked for. And there are examples, which are powerful enough to get their own section in a moment. You rarely need all four at once, for the most standard and vanilla of tasks we could probably write the prompt quite promptly, the instruction may be all we need. If it's a very common task it will probably be very easy to word. For more bespoke tasks you probably won't get the wording right on the first try, especially if you're new to working with LLMs. In that case we just have to send our best attempt, read what comes back, notice the one place we were vague, and tighten it. Much of prompt engineering is just that simple loop.
heading: Prompting: Slowly and Promptly

text: Imagine that we're trying to code up a fun game of soccer. We might need to get the rules into a format that can be parsed by our programming language of choice, let's say Python. Watch what happens when we ask the same factual question three slightly different ways.
heading: Prompting: Slowly and Promptly

text: The first time we just ask for a sentence, and the model gives back a correct, readable line of English for people, not programs, so there's no field to reach into. The second time we ask for a list, and the lines look ready to read, but the model picks its own human-style labels, so reaching for fields['playersperside'] raises a KeyError. Only the third time is safe. We spell out the exact key names, ask for bare numbers, and tell it to add nothing else, and now a tiny key: value parser reads every field by name. The lesson here is that when you need a specific shape back, pin down every part of it. The model gives you back just what you nailed down, and improvises everything you leave loose.
heading: Prompting: Slowly and Promptly

text: Examples turn out to be powerful enough to organize a whole family of prompting styles, sorted by how many examples you hand the model. These are very intuitive so we'll go through them quickly. Zero-shot means asking the model to do something without giving it any examples. We rely entirely on what it learned during training.
heading: Zero-Shot and Few-Shot Learning

text: Zero-shot works surprisingly well for many common tasks. Modern LLMs have been trained on so much text that they've encountered most tasks before in some form. But it has real limits: complex, multi-step problems will often go wrong in this mode. Also, if the task could be interpreted multiple ways, the model will pick one, and possibly not the one you wanted.
heading: Zero-Shot and Few-Shot Learning

text: When zero-shot isn't enough, we can add a few examples. Giving the model exactly one example is called one-shot prompting, and giving it a small handful is few-shot prompting. The discovery that large models can pick up a brand-new task from just a few examples sitting in the prompt, with no retraining at all, was one of the headline results of the GPT-3 paper . Researchers have since traced this in-context learning ability down to specific attention circuits that form during training . The model doesn't learn from these examples in the training sense. Its weights don't change, but it picks up the established pattern and continues it.
heading: Zero-Shot and Few-Shot Learning

text: A few-shot prompt is more explicit about the pattern. Three to six examples is usually the sweet spot. Too few and the pattern is ambiguous. Too many and they're redundant, wasting context window space. Let's look at a college CS professor's template for classifying student emails.
heading: Zero-Shot and Few-Shot Learning

text: The few-shot examples do the heavy lifting here: they teach the model our classification vocabulary and the expected output format without any explicit rule-writing. A few labeled examples are often enough for a simple three-class problem. Let's run the template against a small batch of new student questions.
heading: Zero-Shot and Few-Shot Learning

text: What's most important when putting together your examples? Quality beats quantity: one excellent, unambiguous example beats several mediocre ones. If your task has several types of inputs, include an example of each. And order can matter because some models are sensitive to example ordering. .
heading: Zero-Shot and Few-Shot Learning

text: Whatever you do, don't picture a pink elephant! You pictured the elephant, everyone does, and it isn't your fault. That instruction has to name the elephant to forbid it, and naming it is what paints it across the front of our minds. Psychologists call this ironic process theory . Language models have the very same blind spot, except for them it isn't a matter of psychology but of mathematics. A negative instruction like "do not mention X" drops the word X right into the prompt, the model reads it like every other word, and the odds it assigns to that exact word climb instead of fall. The cleanest version of the trap: we ask three models a plain factual question and forbid the one word that happens to be the answer.
heading: Zero-Shot and Few-Shot Learning

text: Our most capable model, gemma4, kept its word and never typed "elephant." But notice the price it paid. It called the creature an "African bush behemoth," which isn't a real species. The two smaller models couldn't even manage that. Both of them read the ban, felt the overwhelming pull of the single most likely next word, and caved, saying "elephant" anyway. The probability of that one token was simply too high for a polite "do not" to hold them back. Before we write the smaller models off as being bad, consider that we banned the one word that happened to be the only truthful answer, so the model had nowhere else to go. Let's retry with a bit of fair breathing room.
heading: Zero-Shot and Few-Shot Learning

text: Three different bans, and gemma4 honored every one. It described the ocean without ever saying "water," announced a coffee maker without "coffee" or "machine," and in the last line wrote a whole cheerful sentence without once using the letter "e," which is the sort of constraint that would tie most of us humans in knots. This is especially impressive when you consider that e is the most commonly occurring letter in English . A negative instruction is only as strong as the detour it leaves open. When there's a graceful way around the banned word, the model takes it. When the banned word is the only truthful answer, the way it was for our elephant, the ban either bends the truth or breaks it. We can lean on a negative when the thing you're forbidding is a stray word or a style the model can easily swap out. We prefer positives whenever the thing we're forbidding sis difficult to avoid, so "call it the gentle giant" beats "do not say elephant" every time, because it hands the model somewhere to actually go instead of just eggshells to walk on.
heading: Zero-Shot and Few-Shot Learning

text: We can meaningfully improve a model's performance on reasoning tasks just by asking it to think step by step. This is called Chain of Thought (CoT) prompting, and the simplest version is a single added sentence: "Let's think step by step." . This is appended to the with COT prompts here behind the scenes.
heading: Chain of Thought: Getting the Model to Show Its Work

text: The correct answer, by the way, is 67. When you were 6 your sister was 3, so the age gap is 3, and it stays 3 forever. Qwen3 is the only one that nails it both ways, landing on 67 cold or coached. Gemma and Mistral are the rescue missions: left alone, Gemma blurts out 76 and Mistral 65, but asked to go step by step, each one works its way to 67. Llama initially answers 3, the sister's age way back when you were 6; given room to "reason," it only trades that for another wrong number, landing on 70, our own current age. The extra steps didn't rescue it, they just helped produce a different wrong answer. Chain of thought isn't magic: it gives a capable model room to work, but it can also give a confused model room to spiral.
heading: Chain of Thought: Getting the Model to Show Its Work

text: Why does CoT help at all? When we ask the model to reason step by step, we're asking it to generate intermediate tokens before the final answer, which creates a reasoning scaffold it then uses to arrive at a conclusion. This mechanism is very similar to how so-called Thinking models work, we'll return to this fast-versus-slow distinction later in the Thinking chapter. Why does writing out steps help? Think of long division. If you try to compute 847 ÷ 7 entirely in your head, you're likely to make an error. If you write out each step on paper, you're more likely to get to the right answer. The paper is our working memory. For an LLM, each intermediate reasoning token it generates is a note on the scratch paper. The model reads its own previous tokens as context for the next one, so writing the steps down lets it use them.
heading: Chain of Thought: Getting the Model to Show Its Work

text: LLMs can fall into what gets called misguided attention: the model latches onto surface-level features of a problem ("this looks like a river-crossing puzzle, I know this one!") and stops noticing how the version in front of it is actually a variation of the original problem . It pattern-matches to a familiar problem even when the real question is much simpler. The model has seen many river-crossing puzzles, those patterns light up, and it never registers that this particular puzzle is quite different. The original is so culturally saturated that Google hides a small interactive easter egg behind the search for "wolf goat cabbage riddle" , a little game with a wolf, a goat, and a cabbage you can ferry across the river yourself. Here I deliberately reached for one of the smaller models to make the trap easy to spring. Watch it march through the whole wolf, goat, and cabbage routine, shuttling items back and forth, even though the question explicitly spells out that just the goat needs to cross. I would be remiss if I didn't point out the irony here. The model fails the one thing actually asked.
heading: Chain of Thought: Getting the Model to Show Its Work

text: It doesn't even finish the classic routine correctly: if we trace the moves we see the goat is brought back on the fourth trip and never returned, so gemma's answer ends with the wolf and cabbage safely across and the goat marooned on the original bank, the exact opposite of what we asked for. To be fair, bigger models will resist this particular puzzle more often, though none are completely immune to all puzzles.
heading: Chain of Thought: Getting the Model to Show Its Work

text: Q: Can I trust chain-of-thought reasoning? It can be quite fascinating to read the COT for some thinking or reasoning models, particularly the more paranoid ones that can get quite spawling and convoluted. I encourage you to try this, with deepseek-r1 for example, who is probably the undisputed yap champion. This can be more than a humorous pastime, AI safety researchers will inspect the COT along with the eventual output to check for symptoms of undesirable behaviors like mis-alignment and deception in our machines. However, this can be problematic, because the reasoning the model shows you isn't necessarily the reasoning the model did . Chain of thought looks like a window into how the model arrived at its answer. It's more like a written justification produced in parallel with the answer. The two usually agree, because the same patterns that drive a good answer also drive a good explanation of that answer. But they can come apart. The model can write five paragraphs of clean step-by-step logic and then end with a wrong number, and it'll do this without flagging that anything is off. It can also produce the correct answer and then invent a totally false chain of reasoning to support it.
heading: Chain of Thought: Getting the Model to Show Its Work

text: I think the safest mental model is to treat the reasoning trace as a useful artifact, not as a proof. It's useful because it gives the model more tokens to "think over," which improves accuracy on many problems. It isn't a proof because there's usually no internal mechanism guaranteeing the steps produced the conclusion. Recent work makes this concrete: chain-of-thought explanations can be systematically unfaithful, looking perfectly reasonable while failing to reflect the actual reason the model landed on its answer .
heading: Chain of Thought: Getting the Model to Show Its Work

text: If you haven't run into that notation before, it's shorthand for how the code's work grows as the input grows. O(1) means the amount of work stays the same no matter how long the list is, while O(n) means it grows in step with the list, one step of work per item. The loop makes this function look like the O(n) kind, but it's really the O(1) kind, because it exits on the very first step. Let's put the question to a small general-purpose model and also to one trained specifically on code.
heading: Chain of Thought: Getting the Model to Show Its Work

text: The little general model does exactly what we've come to expect by now. It spots a for loop and reaches for the memorized answer, O(n), never noticing that the function bails out on the very first pass. The model trained on code isn't fooled as easily. It reads that early return and correctly calls it O(1). This demonstrates that misguided attention isn't a fixed property of LLMs, rather it depends on the model and the task.
heading: Chain of Thought: Getting the Model to Show Its Work

text: Chain of thought asks the model to slow down and show its work. A close relative asks for something subtly different: not "show your steps" but "hold on, zoom out, let's see the forest for the trees". Instead of charging straight at a hard question, the model first takes a step back and names the general principle, the law, or the rule that the question is really about. Only then does it work toward the specific answer. This is called step-back prompting . Think about how a strong math or physics student handles a tricky test problem. The weak move is to grab the first numbers in sight and start mashing them together. The strong move is to pause and ask, "what is this problem really asking, and what's the appropriate theorem?" In code it's just two small calls to the model, one stacked on the other. First we ask for the governing principle and nothing else. Then we hand that principle back and ask it to solve the original problem in light of it: Let's watch it on a question about a medicine wearing off, the kind where a quickfire answer tends to wander into the wrong neighborhood.
heading: Step-Back Prompting: Find the Principle First

text: Asked cold, the gemma4 responds 1/4, which is wrong. Then we make it step back. Asked first for the principle, it names exponential decay, the rule that a substance loses half of whatever is left in each half-life. With that rule in hand it's much easier to reason about the answer: twelve hours is three half-lives of four hours each, so the dose is halved three times over, first down to a half, then a quarter, then 1/8 which is correct.
heading: Step-Back Prompting: Find the Principle First

text: One question is just anecdotal, so we ran the same step-back or no contest head-to-head over a handful of physics and chemistry problems, eight tries each way for nondeterminism. The results tell a mixed story. On the left are the wins. The half-life question goes from never right to always right: cold, the model chases the wrong intuition every time, and naming the principle rescues it every time. The pendulum, where the period grows with the square root of the length, is a softer win. Cold, the model is a coin flip, sometimes remembering the square root and sometimes assuming four times the length means four times the swing. Stepping back consistently steadies it to a perfect score. On the pressure problem, where a cooling gas has to lose pressure in step with its temperature, stepping back lifts the model from hopeless up to a coin flip, and no further. The principle points it in the right direction, but the model still trips on the actual arithmetic half the time, often reporting the bare ratio instead of finishing the sum, because naming a law can't conjure a calculation the model simply can't do reliably. And on the right sits the question the model already nails cold.
heading: Step-Back Prompting: Find the Principle First

text: The inverse-square problem is a tie at the ceiling, since there was nothing to fix. So, like COT, step-back is a tool which pays off in a specific situation, here it is when the model knows the right principle but its snap judgment reaches for the wrong one. It does nothing for a question the model can already answer very easily, and it can't save a problem the model can't compute.
heading: Step-Back Prompting: Find the Principle First

text: Everything in this chapter traces back to one idea: a prompt isn't really a question, it's the opening of a pattern the model tries to continue, and every technique we covered is a way of steering that continuation on purpose. We worked the control panel with temperature, top-k, and top-p, taught the model by example with few-shot prompts, and climbed the reasoning ladder from chain of thought to step-back. We have to develop the judgement to recognize which tasks can be rescued by the tools and techniques we discussed in this chapter. By the way: the model never really sees your words at all. Your text gets chopped into chunks called tokens first, and that hidden translation explains a surprising number of model quirks. We'll pick up there in the next chapter, Tokens.
heading: Conclusion

text: 1. Write a problem that looks like a famous puzzle but has a trivial answer, the way "he only needs to get the goat across" hides inside the wolf-goat-cabbage setup. See whether the model pattern-matches to the familiar version and overcomplicates it. Then add "Let's think step by step" and watch whether that rescues the answer or only helps it spiral out more confidently. 2. Find a question where your own first instinct tends to be wrong, the way twelve hours of a four-hour half-life feels like "about a quarter left" until you do the math. Ask the model for just the answer, then ask again but have it first name the principle or formula at work before solving. Notice whether naming the rule changes the answer. 3. Dig up a real prompt you recently gave any chatbot and rewrite it explicitly with the four anatomy parts: role, context, task, format. Note which parts were missing from your original, predict which single missing part would change the output most, then send both versions and compare. 4. Set your own pink elephant traps. Design three negative "do not mention X" instructions of your own where X is strongly associated with the topic, then run each against a small model and the chapter's default model. Score the leaks. Then rewrite the instructions more positively, giving the model a few outs, and compare.
heading: Exercises

text: Further Reading Few-shot prompting at scale was popularized by the GPT-3 paper . Chain-of-thought prompting was formally studied in , and the zero-shot version, just adding "Let's think step by step", was demonstrated to work well in . Self-consistency, which samples several reasoning chains and takes a majority vote, pushes accuracy further still , while is a sober reminder that those reasoning traces can misrepresent what the model really did. For a systematic survey of prompting techniques, including roles, system prompts, and the few-shot methods we used here, see , and for an earlier conceptual treatment of prompt design, see . Several other prompting patterns decompose reasoning in different ways: Self-Ask has the model pose and answer its own follow-up questions before committing to a final answer , Least-to-Most works a hard problem by first solving simpler subproblems and building up to the whole , and Self-Discover lets the model compose a bespoke reasoning structure for a task before it begins .
heading: Exercises

text: Abstract Tokenization is the very first thing that happens to your text before a language model ever sees it, the step that chops words and code into the units a model reads. We pin down what a token is, and we pay special attention to code, where indentation and whitespace carry meaning. We see why the context window makes token efficiency a practical concern.

A language model doesn't read text the way you and I do (it doesn't see images or hear sound like us either, but we'll get to that in the Multimodal chapter). As you might have guessed by now, it really only sees numbers, specifically integer IDs that correspond to chunks of text called tokens. Before any generation happens, every piece of input gets converted into a sequence of these token IDs. That conversion process is called tokenization, and it varies based on how common a word or phrase is in the training data. The natural assumption is that a token equals a word. Well, sometimes it does, but not always! Token structure actually varies depending on the tokenizer (a token building program), that we use. An emoji might be a single token or might not be in the vocabulary at all. The word "cat" is probably a single token with most tokenizers. But "tokenization" might be split into "token" and "ization." The words "antidisestablishmentarianism" and "pneumonoultramicroscopicsilicovolcanoconiosis" get split into several pieces.
heading: What's a Token?

text: Holy Toledo on that last token count. Clearly the word "pneumonoultramicroscopicsilicovolcanoconiosis" did not show up in the training data very much, but maybe it will in the future now I've mentioned it in this book so many times. I probably also significantly inflated the book's token count with that, sorry Sophia. If a model only ever sees just these chunks, then asking how many of a certain letter a word contains is asking it to read something it literally can't see. Let's run a small survey: six words likely to bury a repeated letter inside their chunks, put to four models from tiny to capable, each asked once for a specific letter count.
heading: What's a Token?

text: Six tricky words down the rows, four models across the top, each cell one model's single answer. Green marks a correct count and soft red a miss, with the true answer in the left column and each model's tally below. The grid comes back mostly red.
heading: What's a Token?

text: qwen3 is the Spelling Bee champion with three of six, gemma4 manages two, and the tiny llama3.2:1b and the much larger llama3.1 land just one apiece. raspberry defeats all four at once, and mississippi is the only word more than half the models get right. Each model misses in its own way. The little llama3.2:1b overshoots, mostly fives and sixes where the answer is three or four. gemma4 leans the other way, answering two where the answer is three, over and over. llama3.1 barely counts at all: it replies 4 to every single word on the list and scores its one hit on mississippi only because four happened to be right there. Only qwen3 looks like it's counting at all, and even it trips on raspberry, parallel, and possesses.
heading: What's a Token?

text: The word fragmentation is referred to as: subword tokenization. Common sequences collapse into a single token, which is efficient, while rare or unusual words get broken into pieces, which isn't. A model raised on English prose will swallow the most commonn and short English words whole, yet fragment a specialized terms and lengthy or compound words. Drawn from one end to the other, the trip from a word to the numbers the model consumes looks a bit like this.
heading: What's a Token?

text: The little boxes aren't a rendering bug. The strawberry emoji isn't in the vocabulary as a single piece, so the tokenizer falls back to storing it as three raw bytes, none of which is a printable character on its own. Accented words like café and naïve get broken up too. It's a small preview of a fairness problem we'll come back to: the further your text drifts from common English, the more tokens it costs . Most modern LLMs use Byte Pair Encoding (BPE), or similar algorithms like WordPiece or SentencePiece, to build their vocabulary, and watching it run explains a lot about tokens. BPE starts with individual characters and iteratively merges the most frequently co-occurring pairs. Run it long enough on a large corpus and common words become single tokens, common prefixes and suffixes become tokens, and rare or unseen strings get decomposed into whatever's left.
heading: What's a Token?

text: BPE builds one shared vocabulary for everything a model ever reads, but code leans on that vocabulary very differently than prose does. In ordinary writing, swapping "happy" for "glad" barely makes a real difference. In code, swapping = for == silently breaks your program, so the model's token-level view has to preserve distinctions that prose would happily smudge. snakecase versus camelCase versus kebab-case: each one lands as its own sequence of tokens, and an unusual identifier can splinter into four or five pieces. Before we look at those cases, one practical note for the rest of the chapter. Our counttokens and tokenize helpers default to a modern general-purpose tokenizer, the one behind GPT-4. When a comparison helps, we'll point them at another by name: 'code' for a code-specialized tokenizer (StarCoder2), 'old' for a 2019-era one (GPT-2 ), and 'multilingual' for a newer one built with many languages in mind (GPT-4o). Here's a single line of code, run first through the general-purpose tokenizer and then through the code-specialized one, every token in its own box.
heading: How Code Gets Tokenized

text: The same line through two tokenizers. The general-purpose tokenizer (above) keeps each underscore attached to the word ahead of it, so the name spans eight tokens; the code-specialized StarCoder2 tokenizer (below) gives every underscore a token of its own, so the same name spans thirteen. They diverge because each learned its merges from different training text.
heading: How Code Gets Tokenized

text: The same function renamed in camelCase. With no underscores to break on, both tokenizers fold the capital-letter humps into the same two pieces, getUser and ById, so their counts nearly match: five and six. The one difference is that the code tokenizer splits the opening parenthesis off from userId. The boundary moves with how the name is written, not only with the tokenizer.
heading: How Code Gets Tokenized

text: Different tokenizers strike different bargains here. A code tokenizer often spends a separate token on every underscore or hyphen, so getuserbyid and get-user-by-id cost more tokens than the equivalent getUserById, even though both read the same to us. ALLCAPS names fragment too. If a variable name ever triggers odd behavior in an AI system, its tokenization is a reasonable thing to check.
heading: How Code Gets Tokenized

text: It's tempting to assume a code-specialized tokenizer must always win here. It doesn't. The tokenizers behind StarCoder , CodeLlama , and DeepSeek Coder do bake in dedicated handling for things like long runs of indentation. But modern general-purpose tokenizers have largely caught up on code, because today's models all train on mountains of it, so a dedicated code tokenizer isn't automatically more efficient.
heading: How Code Gets Tokenized

text: Beyond the regular vocabulary, models use special tokens to mark structural information. These aren't words; they're control signals. - <|endoftext|>: Marks the end of a document. - <|fimprefix|>, <|fimsuffix|>, <|fimmiddle|>: Used for fill-in-the-middle tasks, where the model completes code given both what comes before and after. - <pad>, <bos>, <eos>: Padding, beginning-of-sequence, end-of-sequence markers. These structural markers reveal how deliberately these systems are designed. The model isn't just reading text. It's reading text with embedded structure signals. The code tokenizer we've been using reserves thirty-eight of them.
heading: Special Tokens and What They Signal

text: Every reserved control token in StarCoder2's vocabulary, grouped by what it marks. Most have nothing to do with code itself: they label the parts of a software project the model trained on, including issue threads, pull-request diffs and reviews, Jupyter notebook cells, and even redacted secrets like passwords and API keys.
heading: Special Tokens and What They Signal

text: The online code repository (GitHub etc.) pull-request family alone claims sixteen of the thirty-eight, close to half the reserved set. What filled those training sets wasn't polished code so much as the back-and-forth around it: people proposing changes, reviewing each other's work, and signing off. This is the scaffolding intentionally wrapped around millions of scraped projects so the downstream models could learn tell a commit apart from a review reply.
heading: Special Tokens and What They Signal

text: Q: Is a tokenizer learned from data, or built by hand? Both. The vocabulary itself is learned without any labels. Byte Pair Encoding starts from single characters and merges the most frequent neighboring pair over and over until it reaches a target size, while close relatives like WordPiece and the Unigram approach in SentencePiece trade raw frequency for a likelihood score. The scaffolding around that pass is set by humans. The vocabulary size, the text normalization, and the special tokens above: no counting procedure invented <|endoftext|> or <PASSWORD>, someone reserved them intentionally to mark structure that people cared about.
heading: Special Tokens and What They Signal

text: Python's significant whitespace, which defines scope, is one of the trickier things for tokenizers to handle correctly. We can watch this by taking a single deeply indented line, return value, and running it through three tokenizers, with every space drawn as a dot so the indentation is visible. Each row prints how many tokens the line breaks into and the pieces themselves. The 2019-era GPT-2 tokenizer spends a separate token on every single space, so twelve spaces of nesting 3 levels deep cost twelve tokens before any real code begins. A code-first tokenizer like StarCoder2 folds that whole run into one token, and a modern general-purpose tokenizer does the same. The indentation that makes Python readable is nearly free to a current tokenizer, and quite expensive to an old one.
heading: Indentation and Whitespace

text: The context window is the maximum number of tokens a model can process at once. It's the model's workbench: it can only "see" what's currently on the table. Everything that needs to influence the model's output, including the system prompt, the conversation history, any code, documents or images you paste in, and the model's own previous responses, has to fit within that window. This makes token efficiency a practical concern. More tokens in the prompt means less room for the model's response. Also, longer outputs use more tokens and cost more if you're using a paid API. And fragmented tokenization (unusual variable names, rare terminology) uses your context window less efficiently than clean tokenization does. How big is a context window in practice? As demonstrated below, one token is roughly four to five characters of English text, or only two or three characters of code . So a model with a 128,000-token context window can hold roughly a 300-page novel, or the entire source code of a small application. Models keep getting larger context windows, with some of the largest now in the scale of millions of tokens .
heading: Token Count and the Context Window

text: One number for the whole book, and it lands well under the 128,000-token window from the rule of thumb above. A mid-sized model could, in principle, read everything you're holding in a single "sitting". Whether it should is another matter. Stuffing a window to its limit also comes with a catch: models attend most reliably to what sits at the very start and end of the context and tend to lose track of material buried in the middle, a failure documented as the lost in the middle problem . There are ways to fit more into that budget. Prompt compression trims a wordy prompt down to the tokens that carry the meaning, so the model reads less without losing the point . We will discuss this further in the Efficiency chapter.
heading: Token Count and the Context Window

text: Q: What's a context window? Is it like the model's memory? "Memory" is a tempting word but a misleading one. A real memory persists across conversations and grows over time. The model's context window does neither. The truth is that a language model has no memory at all. It's a stateless function. Every time you press send, the entire conversation, every message you ever wrote and every reply it ever gave, is bundled up and shoved through the model from scratch as if it were a single long prompt. The context window is just the maximum length of that bundle.
heading: Token Count and the Context Window

text: gemma4 says 24818827 but the actual answer is 24791107 How does gemma4 mess this up? Let's think about how multiplication typically works. We line the numbers up in columns: ones under ones, tens under tens, and work from the right. Every step needs one specific digit you can point to, like below. You'd multiply 3947 by the 1, then the 8, then the 2, then the 6, shifting and adding. Each step needs a single digit on its own. Now recall how our tokenizer chops a number into chunks of three digits, counted from the left. This means that if we're multiplying numbers with different digit lengths, the numeric blocks won't line up. But even if it's four digits evenly for each number, the model never receives those eight digits. The tokenizer hands it two opaque chunks per number: 394 is the digits 3, 9, 4 fused into one token. The model can't reach inside it to grab "the 9" or "the 4," so it can't run the column-by-column procedure, the individual digits it would multiply never arrive as separate things. Stuck with two blobs, it falls back on pattern-matching: it emits a number that looks about right (eight digits, starts near 24 million) instead of one it computed.
heading: How Tokenization Affects Generation Quality

text: That's the result in the demo: the true product is 24,791,107, and gemma4 confidently returns 24,818,827, which is the right size, and has the right leading digits, but is the wrong number. This, along with the other token-caused quirks we've seen in this chapter, can be considered a failure of perception, not reasoning.
heading: How Tokenization Affects Generation Quality

text: Tokenization is the translation layer between human text and the numbers a language model works with. It shapes almost everything downstream: what the model can see, why it can't reliably count the letters in strawberry, how much of your context window real content gets to use, math and logic reasoning errors, etc. You don't need to think about it constantly, but when a model does something baffling, tokenization is often one place worth looking. Something is still missing, though: a token is nothing but an integer ID, so how does the model learn that the token for "king" has anything to do with the token for "queen"? That's the question Semantics takes on, when each token becomes a point in space and distance represents meaning.
heading: Conclusion

text: 1. Run counttokens and tokenize on a handful of strings: your own name, a long technical term from your field, a common English word, and an emoji. Before you look at each result, guess whether it'll be a single clean token or shatter into several pieces, then check how often your intuition was right. Ask the model to spell each word backward and see whether it succeeds. 2. For each of these tasks, decide on paper whether tokenization is the reason a model finds it hard: counting the letters in a word, rhyming two words, dividing two arbitrary length numbers. 3. Compare what prose and code cost. Take a paragraph of plain English and a Python snippet of about the same length on screen, and run counttokens on both. Predict the winner first, then use what the indentation section showed to explain the result. Try the same snippet with tabs converted to spaces and see whether the bill changes.
heading: Exercises

text: Further Reading Byte Pair Encoding was introduced to NLP by and extended in . The context window constraint is a recurring theme in applied work with LLMs; discusses how model capability scales with both compute and context. For a deep dive into how tokenization shapes model behavior on code specifically, is a good starting point. The uneven cost tokenization imposes across human languages is documented carefully in , and shows that the choice of tokenizer measurably changes how well a multilingual model trains in the first place.
heading: Exercises

text: Abstract One of the most interesting ideas in modern AI is that the meaning of a word can be captured by its location in space. We start with the distributional hypothesis, the observation that you can learn what a word means by watching the company it keeps, and we turn that into geometry, where 'similar' becomes 'close together' and cosine similarity measures this precisely. We trace how embeddings grow from single words to whole passages, and put the same idea to work on semantic search and document clustering.

The meaning of a word is its use in the language. — Ludwig Wittgenstein, Philosophical Investigations, 1953 You shall know a word by the company it keeps. — J.R. Firth, "A Synopsis of Linguistic Theory, 1930--1955," in Studies in Linguistic Analysis, 1957

An embedding is a way of turning something discrete, like a word or a sentence, into a point in space. We train a model so that things with similar meanings land near each other and things with unrelated meanings land far apart. Concretely, an embedding maps any object to a list of numbers.
heading: Words as Locations

text: That vector produced by nomic-embed-text (our embedding model of choice) is 768 numbers for a single three-letter word. Eyeballing the numbers tells us almost nothing about whether "cat" sits closer to "kitten" or to "dog." We need a principled way to measure how close two of these points are, which is where cosine similarity comes in a little later.
heading: Words as Locations

text: First, understanding why we need embeddings is important. Consider these two sentences: 1. "The cat sat on the mat." 2. "A feline rested on the rug." Any human reading those sentences immediately knows they mean nearly the same thing. But a computer counting shared words sees only two words in common, "the" and "on." Measured against the nine distinct words across the two sentences, that's a similarity score of about 0.2. This bag-of-words approach treats every word as completely unrelated to every other word. "Cat" and "feline" are treated as being no more similar than "cat" and "economics." We need something better.
heading: Why Word-Counting Fails

text: Once we have vectors, how do we measure how similar two of them are? The most common approach is cosine similarity, which measures the angle between two vectors. Think of two arrows on a piece of paper. If both arrows point the same direction, they're perfectly similar, a score of 1. If they sit at right angles, ninety degrees apart, they have nothing in common, a score of 0. And if two arrows point in exactly opposite directions, they mean opposite things, a score of -1.
heading: Measuring Similarity

text: The ranking lines up with common sense: kitten sits closest to cat, dog comes next, and car is furthest. That 0.558 doesn't mean cat and car are halfway alike; it just means car is the least catlike of the three.
heading: Measuring Similarity

text: Words that show up in similar contexts end up with similar vectors, and vice versa. This is the definition of the so-called distributional hypothesis. So when we say two sentences are "semantically similar," what we really mean is that the words in them have historically lived in similar neighborhoods of human writing. This does not always match and intuitive notion of semantics, for example certain word pairings with semantic relationships such as antonyms (words with opposite meanings) actually do appear together quite frequently and are considered similar, as we'll soon see demonstrated.
heading: Measuring Similarity

text: Each word is a real vector with hundreds of dimensions. We can't plot hundreds of axes directly, but we can project them down to two dimensions using a technique called t-SNE (t-Distributed Stochastic Neighbor Embedding). Things that were semantically close in the high-dimensional space stay close in the 2D picture. The exact coordinates don't mean anything, but the clusters do.
heading: Measuring Similarity

text: Q: Why do real embeddings need hundreds of dimensions? In two dimensions, "happy" and "sad" must sit on opposite sides, but where do you put "formal," "sarcastic," "Italian," "medieval," and "about food"? They all have to share the same plane and they end up stepping on each other. Add hundreds of dimensions and every one of those properties can have its own direction. The model can encode many cross-cutting properties of meaning at once, and the geometry stays clean enough that similarity comparisons still work. The number 768 itself isn't magical. It's an engineering tradeoff between how much nuance the model can carry and how much storage and compute we're willing to spend.
heading: Measuring Similarity

text: One of the most remarkable properties of word embeddings is that they support arithmetic, for example if you take the vector for "king," subtract the vector for "man," and add the vector for "woman," you land close to the vector for "queen" . That demo is charming, and we can reproduce it. In a moment we'll watch king minus man plus woman land squarely on "queen," and the same move handle grammar just as well, climbing from one comparative to the next. The trick is real, but it's oversold: ask it for an opposite and the geometry that nailed "queen" hands back the wrong word with the same confidence. Also these 'analogies' happily reproduce the biases baked into their training text . We can test all of this with nomic-embed-text. If embeddings capture relationships like "royalty" and "gender" as geometric directions, the arithmetic should work out.
heading: Word Arithmetic

text: The first three lines follow the plot. King minus man plus woman lands on "queen," and the same move handles grammar just as cleanly: the comparative step from "good" to "better," dropped on "bad," gives "worse," and that same step carries "small" to "smaller." It seems so far like the relationship we pull out of one pair really does transfer to another. The fourth line breaks, and it breaks in the wrong direction. We asked for the opposite of "rich" and the model handed back "wealthy," a synonym, at 0.73 similarity between the vector the arithmetic produced and "wealthy," the best candidate. The arithmetic moved the wrong way entirely from "poor". That seems strange, because the cosine scale had a slot ready for opposites. Two vectors pointing in opposite directions score -1. So perhaps the question of why our last experiment failed can be answered by checking: where do opposites actually tend to land on that scale?
heading: Word Arithmetic

text: Nowhere near -1. Every opposite pair scores high and positive, from "rich" and "poor" at 0.62 up to "true" and "false" at 0.86, and all of them sit above two unrelated words like "happy" and "economics" at 0.41. Opposite in meaning turns out to be close in space.
