Again, this is the distributional hypothesis in action: a foundational idea in linguistics and natural language processing (NLP), which states that words that occur in similar contexts tend to have similar meanings. A word's vector is built from the company it keeps, and "happy" and "sad" keep almost identical company: "I felt so happy after the call" and "I felt so sad after the call". Words that fill the same slots point the same way, and "true" and "false," which travel together more than any other pair here, sit highest of all. Landing at -1 would take two words that are opposite in the sense that they never share a context, this is our new semantics. The arithmetic works by borrowing an arrow. The step from "man" to "woman" is the same step as the one from "king" to "queen," so you can measure it on one pair and drop it on the other.
heading: Word Arithmetic

text: Gender is an arrow the whole vocabulary shares, and so is comparison: the step from "big" to "bigger" is the step from "tall" to "taller." Opposites have no such shared arrow. The step from "happy" to "sad" points almost nowhere near the step from "rich" to "poor," because dimming a mood and draining a fortune are different moves through the space, so the arrow we borrow from "happy" and "sad" drops us nowhere near "poor."
heading: Word Arithmetic

text: Each relationship's step measured for two word pairs and drawn from a shared origin, separated by the true angle between them. Gender and comparison hold their arrows in a cone, so a step lifted from one pair still points roughly the right way at another. The opposite steps swing apart toward a right angle, each word negating along its own attribute.
heading: Word Arithmetic

text: For gender and comparison, the arithmetic works perfectly. "Man" to "woman" and "king" to "queen" agree at 0.54, "man" to "woman" and "actor" to "actress" at 0.71, and even the loosest comparison pair at 0.30 still beats every opposite on the list. The opposite steps don't agree with each other at all: "happy" to "sad" against "rich" to "poor" is 0.19, and against "hot" to "cold" it's 0.04, a clean right angle. In the figure the gender and comparison arrows stay bunched on one side while the opposite arrows fan out toward the perpendicular. Gender is a single arrow because man-versus-woman is the same contrast as king-versus-queen, the identical swap wherever it's applied. "Opposite" isn't a single contrast. It picks a different attribute for every word: the opposite of "happy" runs along mood, of "rich" along wealth, of "hot" along temperature. Dimming a mood and draining a fortune are different moves through the space, so the arrow we lift from "happy" and "sad" points nowhere near "poor" once we set it down at "rich."
heading: Word Arithmetic

text: That's noticeably better than counting shared words. But averaging word vectors throws away word order and grammar. "The dog bit the cat" and "The cat bit the dog" contain identical words and get identical averaged embeddings, even though they describe very different events. Embedding the whole sentence at once does pull the two apart, which is the right call since the meaning flips, if just barely:
heading: Sentence Embeddings

text: The difference is in the gap. Averaging rates the unrelated sentence almost as high as the paraphrase, 0.95 against 0.86, leaving a gap of just 0.09. The transformer pulls them apart, 0.72 against 0.47, a gap of 0.25 that's nearly three times as wide.
heading: Sentence Embeddings

text: The five test cases. Each one is three sentences: an anchor, a paraphrase of it, and an off-topic line. The anchor is the fixed reference, compared with the paraphrase for the related score and with the off-topic line for the unrelated score, so each score is its own pairwise comparison and the two simply share that anchor.
heading: Sentence Embeddings

text: Each row of the chart is one case. The orange dot is the anchor's score against its unrelated sentence, the blue dot its score against the related paraphrase, and the line between them is the gap. Under averaging, both dots crowd near the top: every sentence looks broadly alike, so related and unrelated sit almost on top of each other and the lines barely register. The transformer drops the unrelated dot toward 0.4 while holding the related dot high, opening each line to several times its averaged width. Read down either panel and the split holds on all five pairs, not just the cat sentences we started with.
heading: Sentence Embeddings

text: So far we've climbed from words to sentences. Real documents are longer, and here we hit a wall. Every embedding model has a context window, a hard limit on how much text it can read at once. Feed it a whole book and it'll silently truncate everything past the cutoff, embedding only the opening and ignoring the rest. The usual fix is chunking. We slice a long document into passages that fit comfortably inside the window, embed each chunk on its own, and store them all. When someone searches, we match against the chunks rather than the whole document. This is the machinery behind retrieval-augmented generation, which we build in the Augmentation chapter.
heading: Sentence Embeddings

text: Semantics and embeddings underpin the most popular language-based tools of our time. Traditional search matches keywords exactly. Semantic search matches meaning. You can ask a question in your own words and the system finds relevant documents even when they share none of your vocabulary.
heading: Applications: Where Embeddings Show Up

text: Q: Can semantic search fail in ways keyword search would catch? Yes, and the failures are often subtle enough to look like correct answers, which makes them even worse. Keyword search has the courtesy to fail loudly. If you search for "Taylor Swift" and no document contains the name "Taylor Swift," you get zero results and you know you missed the mark. Semantic search will happily return the top three "most similar" documents, ranked by cosine similarity, even when none of them are about Taylor Swift. Maybe they're about Olivia Rodrigo, or about breakup albums, or just about pop. Semantic search can also lose specificity. A query like "Taylor Swift's 2021 re-recording of Red" gets blurred into "Taylor Swift, album re-releases, the music industry," and you lose the very precision that made the query useful in the first place. In practice, the most reliable retrieval systems are hybrid. They use keyword search to anchor on the exact terms that have to match, and embeddings to catch the semantic neighbors the keywords would miss.
heading: Applications: Where Embeddings Show Up

text: Testing AI with AI Generative models can be hard to test, because there's no single correct string of words. Ask a model to summarize an article and a thousand different summaries could all be fine, so a plain equality check, output == expected, is useless. Embeddings give us a way out. We embed the model's answer and a reference answer, measure the cosine similarity between them, and check that it clears some threshold.
heading: Applications: Where Embeddings Show Up

text: Words that keep each other company tend to mean similar things. That one observation, scaled up to billions of sentences and hundreds of embedding dimensions, unlocks everything we built in this chapter: cosine similarity, nearest neighbors, clustering, and semantic search. The next chapter, Metacoding, points the same embeddings at source code, and we watch a model recognize that two programs do the same job even when they don't share a single variable name. The distributional hypothesis, it turns out, reads Python just as happily as it reads prose.
heading: Conclusion

text: 1. Put similarity to the test on meaning versus word overlap. Find a pair of sentences that share almost no words but mean the same thing, like "She aced the exam" and "He passed the test with flying colors," and a pair that shares nearly every word but flips the meaning, like "I love this movie" and "I don't love this movie." Predict each score before you run it, then check whether the embedding rates meaning over surface wording, and see where the negation pair lands given how close opposites sat earlier in the chapter. 2. Confirm the famous trick, then break it on an opposite. Run wordanalogy('king', 'man', 'woman', ['queen', 'prince', 'princess', 'duke']) and watch it land on "queen." Then ask it for an opposite: wordanalogy('happy', 'sad', 'rich', ['poor', 'wealthy', 'money', 'broke']) returns "wealthy," a synonym of "rich," moving the wrong way entirely. To see what went wrong, call showoppositecosines and notice that real opposites like "rich" and "poor" score around 0.62, high and positive, nowhere near the -1 the cosine scale reserves for them. Write a sentence on how two words that mean opposite things can sit so close together in the space. 3. Measure the borrowed arrow the trick depends on.
heading: Exercises

text: An analogy works by lifting the step from one pair and dropping it on another, so the step from "man" to "woman" has to match the step from "king" to "queen." Compute that step as embed("woman") - embed("man"), do the same for embed("queen") - embed("king"), and compare the two with similarity. Then try a relationship with no shared arrow: "happy" to "sad" against "rich" to "poor" (or call showoffsetconsistency for the full table). Notice that the gender steps point roughly the same way while the opposite steps swing toward a right angle, and write a sentence on why "opposite" can't be a single direction the way "gender" can. 4. Reproduce the word-order blind spot, then find where the transformer actually pays off. Take "The dog bit the cat" and "The cat bit the dog" and run them through averagedsimilarity: the score comes back at a flat 1.000, because averaging the word vectors throws away order and the two sentences use identical words. Run the same pair through similarity, which reads the whole sentence at once, and notice it barely separates them either, landing near 0.98.
heading: Exercises

text: The real payoff shows up elsewhere: take a paraphrase that shares few words ("The cat sat on the mat" and "A feline rested on the rug") against an off-topic line, score the related-minus-unrelated gap both ways, and watch the transformer's gap open nearly three times wider than averaging's. Write a sentence on what the transformer can use that the average threw away. 5. Build a small semantic search you can fool. Write a corpus of six to eight short sentences spanning two or three topics, then run semanticsearch with a query that shares none of the exact words of the document you're hoping to surface, and confirm it still finds the right one. Then write a query designed to mislead it, the way searching for one car brand can surface documents about a rival, and notice that the system ranks its top three confidently and never warns you it missed. Decide where you'd add a keyword check to catch what the embeddings blur together. 6. Cluster a corpus, then poke the boundary. Gather nine or ten short sentences from three topics you know well, embed them, and run the same KMeans clustering the chapter used to see whether the groups match the topics you had in mind.
heading: Exercises

text: Now add one sentence that straddles two of your topics, a recipe written like a chemistry procedure, say, and see which cluster claims it. The clean cases sort themselves; write a sentence on what the geometry does when a document belongs to two groups at once. 7. Test AI with AI, and pick the threshold. Ask a model the same factual question twice at a normal temperature, embed both answers with embed, and measure their similarity. Then compare one of those answers against the answer to a completely different question. You now have a "same meaning" score and a "different meaning" score, so choose the threshold you'd put in an automated test that decides whether two answers agree, and write a sentence on what breaks if you set it too low and what breaks if you set it too high.
heading: Exercises

text: Further Reading The Word2Vec papers that opened the modern embedding era are and , and GloVe (Global Vectors for Word Representation) followed from at Stanford. Modern sentence-level embeddings owe much to , whose Sentence-BERT paper showed that transformer models fine-tuned on sentence pairs produce far more useful embeddings than generic pooled representations. Today, embedding models power every major search engine, document retrieval system, and recommendation algorithm in production. OpenAI's text-embedding-3-large, Nomic's nomic-embed-text, and Cohere's embed-v3 all operate on the same fundamental principle Firth described in a lecture more than sixty years ago.
heading: Exercises

text: Abstract This chapter turns the tools of generative AI on code itself, teaching machines to understand the programs that other machines run. We start with code embeddings, which represent a program as a point in a continuous vector space, and use them to build a semantic code search engine that finds the right function even when your query doesn't share a single keyword with it. Along the way we see what makes code different from prose, why code syntax and semantics aren't the same thing, and where code embeddings break down. The second half turns to AI that writes code: how these models are trained, how fast different open-source options generate, and how much to trust what they produce.

Here, instead of pointing a model at human language, we point it at programs themselves and ask it to read, search, compare, and eventually write them, treating source code as just another kind of language it can learn the patterns of. We spent the last chapter, Semantics, learning how language models build a geometric map of human language. Every word and sentence gets placed as a point in a high-dimensional space, and proximity in that space corresponds to similarity in meaning. That same idea, carries over directly to code. Here's a small puzzle: two functions. They look nothing alike. Different names, different lengths, different style. One spells out a loop step by step, the other hands the whole job to Python's built-in sum. And yet they compute exactly the same answer. Any programmer would recognize them as twins in a few seconds. Now here are two more. Maybe we're a programmer for a soccer club trying to create the roster this year for both the men's team and the under-21s. These two look almost identical, but they are opposites They're one character apart. But that one character flips everything: the first lets adults through, the second turns adults away. They are opposites. So here's the question running underneath this whole chapter.
heading: Teaching Machines to Understand Themselves

text: When we ask a model whether two pieces of code are "similar," what exactly is it measuring? Does it see the first pair as equivalent and the second pair as opposites, the way we just did? Or does it get fooled by how the code looks on the surface? The answer has real consequences for us if we plan to trust a machine to search for or even write code.
heading: Teaching Machines to Understand Themselves

text: Code has properties that generic text models can miss or mishandle. Operators carry enormous weight. Changing < to > is a single character, but it reverses the logic of an entire function. Indentation is structural in languages like Python. Variable names carry semantic signals: a function that operates on a parameter named age is probably doing something age-related, even if no official documentation says so. These observations motivated researchers to build embeddings specifically designed for code . The model we use throughout this chapter is UniXcoder, a model from Microsoft trained on millions of code repositories across multiple programming languages.
heading: What Makes Code Different from Prose

text: Here are fifteen Python functions from four different categories, each one embedded by UniXcoder into a 768-dimensional vector and then projected down to two dimensions using PCA so we can look at it. We see PCA again, and are reminded that it is a way of squashing those numerous dimensions down to two so the whole set fits on a page, like casting the shadow of a complicated wire sculpture onto a wall: we lose some depth, but the overall shape and which pieces sit close together still come through. If the model is doing its job, similar functions should appear near each other.
heading: What Makes Code Different from Prose

text: The file I/O functions land far to the right, clearly isolated from everything else. They share a structural pattern, opening a file handle, that UniXcoder evidently treats as a strong signal. The arithmetic functions cluster in the lower left, while strings and lists mingle somewhat in the upper area. That overlap is informative: many list operations and string operations have similar shapes, iteration and transformation over a sequence.
heading: What Makes Code Different from Prose

text: Let's try comparing an iterative sum function to a one-line sum function and a multiplication function, using cosine similarity from UniXcoder code embeddings. The codesimilarity helper does this in two small steps. First it runs each snippet through UniXcoder to get its 768-number vector, then it measures the cosine of the angle between the two vectors, the same method we built up in the Semantics chapter. A score near 1.0 means the vectors point in almost the same direction, and a score near 0.0 means they have little in common.
heading: Syntax vs. Semantics

text: The two sum functions score lower on similarity than sum and multiply do. UniXcoder sees structural shape as well as semantics. The iterative sum and the one-liner sum(lst) look structurally very different: one is a loop with accumulated state, the other is a single-line return. The model picks up on that shape difference. Meanwhile sum and multiply share nearly identical structure, differing only in one operator. The model notices the structural kinship even though the semantics differ. This is intentional. Both semantic and structural similarity matters a great deal in code search and refactoring tasks.
heading: Syntax vs. Semantics

text: Here we see the true importance of structural similarity, clearly illustrated. The same function updated with types added or renamed variables is still the same function and our similarity check indicates as such with high similarity scores. With different logic, not so much. Structural similarity shines when our model can look past the noise of arbitrary variable names and focus on the shape of the code: number of arguments, return type, loop nesting. Two functions can differ completely in their identifiers and still be structurally (and semantically) equivalent.
heading: Syntax vs. Semantics

text: The next snippet tests the boundary case where one function passes and one fails the same logical condition. These two validateage functions are a single operator apart, but that one operator flips the result for every input. The real question is whether an embedding model feels the difference, so let's put UniXcoder and a general-purpose text model, nomic-embed-text from the last chapter, on the exact same pair.
heading: Syntax vs. Semantics

text: Notice which one is worse: nomic-embed-text scores the flip at 0.9949, higher than UniXcoder's 0.9662, so the generic model is less able to feel the difference. When the entire distinction between two functions rides on a single operator, the code-specialist model comes out favorite, as expected.
heading: Syntax vs. Semantics

text: Code embeddings support arithmetic the same way word embeddings do. The classic king - man + woman = queen works because the space encodes directions you can add and subtract. Code embeddings let us play the same game. If there's a direction in the space that means "convert from recursive to iterative style," we should be able to add that arrow to a brand-new recursive function and land near its iterative twin. One sample isn't enough for code though. With just a single pair, the transformation vector gets contaminated by details specific to those two functions. Averaging across multiple pairs cancels the noise out, leaving behind the signal we care about. Our codeanalogy mechanism needs three things. The first is a list of pairs, where each pair is a before-and-after example of the transformation we have in mind, like a few recursive functions sitting next to its iterative rewrites. The second is a query, the new function you want to transform. The third is a list of candidates, the possible answers/outcomes to choose from. Informally, the function embeds every pair and subtracts each before-vector from its after-vector to get a little arrow, then averages those arrows into one clean transformation direction. It then adds that direction to the query's embedding to predict where the answer ought to live, and returns whichever candidate sits closest to that predicted spot, together with its similarity score.
heading: Vector Arithmetic Over Code

text: The analogy pairs are chosen to test whether the model understands the recursive pattern or merely surface-matches on keywords. The candidates include both a structurally similar iterative alternative and a red herring, so we can see how the ranking handles the trade-off.
heading: Vector Arithmetic Over Code

text: Let's line up three code models and run them on the exact same query and snippets. CodeBERT and UniXcoder are both from Microsoft. The third, which I'll call CodeSearch, is a DistilRoBERTa model fine-tuned on CodeSearchNet , a large dataset of real "plain-English question, matching code" pairs. This means, as its name implies, that it was trained specifically on the search task itself, not merely exposed to a lot of raw code.
heading: Vector Arithmetic Over Code

text: Before reading the scores, it helps to say what the ideal answer is. The query is "combine two numbers", and two functions fit that description especially well: add and multiply, each taking exactly two numbers. sumlist is close but takes a list (which might have exactly two numbers in it by good fortune), and readfile and sendemail are completely irrelevant to the search. So the ordering we want is the two two-number functions on top, sumlist in the middle, and the I/O functions clearly excluded. UniXcoder gets the assignment but blurs the top choices: it rates sumlist (0.30) above add and multiply (both 0.22), though it does keep the irrelevant functions lower. CodeBERT is worst here, it crams every score into a thin 0.79 to 0.89 band and ranks the two least relevant functions, sendemail and readfile, right at the top. CodeSearch is the one that best matches our expectations. It puts multiply (0.55) and add (0.50) on top as a clear pair, drops sumlist to 0.31, and pushes the irrelevant functions far down, even scoring readfile below zero. It doesn't single out add over multiply, but neither would a careful person, since both combine two numbers equally well. What it gets unmistakably right is the grouping: the relevant functions cleanly above the irrelevant ones.
heading: Vector Arithmetic Over Code

text: CodeSearch is a smaller DistilRoBERTa whose edge is that it was fine-tuned on the retrieval task itself, on real question-and-code pairs. When you reach for a specialized model, the question isn't necessarily "was it trained on my domain" but "was it trained on my task".
heading: Vector Arithmetic Over Code

text: So far we've been asking what machines can understand about code. Now let's flip the question: what can they generate? Different models in the wild represent different design philosophies. Qwen2.5-Coder comes from Alibaba and is one of the strongest 7B instruction-following models available. CodeLlama is Meta's adaptation of Llama 2 for code , one of the foundational open models in this space. DeepSeek-Coder is a compact 1.3B model from DeepSeek that's notable for its speed . StarCoder2 is a community effort from BigCode, trained on The Stack v2 , a massive curated collection of open-source repositories. DeepCoder is a 1.5B reasoning model , a smaller relative of DeepSeek-R1 that thinks through problems step by step before it writes. These models learn differently from general-purpose language models. The foundation is pretraining on enormous amounts of source code scraped from public repositories, which teaches the model syntax, idioms, and common patterns across many languages. Most coding models also use a technique called fill-in-the-middle training : the model is shown the beginning and end of a code block and asked to fill in what belongs in the gap. This makes them particularly good at code completion, which is a very common real-world use case.
heading: The Machine That Writes The Code

text: Finally, instruction-tuned variants like Qwen2.5-Coder and DeepCoder go through a second phase where they're trained on pairs of instructions and responses, teaching them to follow prompts rather than just complete them. That last step is what separates a model that completes your existing code from one that can answer "write me a function that does X."
heading: The Machine That Writes The Code

text: Speed matters of course but this metric alone doesn't tell the whole story. We paid for those extra parameters in Qwen2.5-Coder in generation speed. The question is whether we got our value in output quality. But quality is more nuanced than "quantity". The classic evaluation mechanism for generated text, BLEU, simply counts overlapping phrases against a reference answer , and it works poorly for code, where two correct solutions can share barely a token. Let's give all five models the same coding task and look at what comes back.
heading: The Machine That Writes The Code

text: Quality is harder to pin down than speed. A function can look textbook-perfect and still be wrong, so reading the code isn't enough. We have to run it. I sent all five models the same prompt and saved exactly what each one wrote, cleaned up with the extractcode helper above. The next cell grades those saved functions by running each one against a battery of known primes and composites and counting how many it gets right.
heading: The Machine That Writes The Code

text: Three of the five models, Qwen2.5-Coder, CodeLlama, and DeepCoder, passed all twelve cases. Each one landed on essentially the same clean, idiomatic solution: The other two are where it gets interesting, because both produced code that looks perfectly reasonable at a glance. StarCoder2 scored 5 out of 12 with this: Look at that else. The loop returns on its very first step regardless of the remainder, so the function calls every number from three upward composite. It gets the composites right for exactly that reason, and it handles 2 correctly because the loop never runs, but it rejects every prime above two. StarCoder2 is a code completion model rather than an instruction follower. Give it a function body to finish and it shines; hand it an open-ended request like this one and it improvises badly. DeepSeek-Coder, the fastest model in the entire benchmark, scored zero. Its base case is backward, so it marks 0 and 1 as prime, and then it runs out of room mid-line and ends on a bare return Tr, a name that doesn't exist, so it crashes the moment you call it. DeepCoder spent over a thousand tokens thinking before it answered, where Qwen2.5-Coder needed only a few dozen, but once we strip the <think> block what remains is correct.
heading: The Machine That Writes The Code

text: One prompt on a classic problem isn't a comprehensive evaluation, just an illustration. The exact scores would shift on other tasks. What this does show, is that the gap between code that looks right and code that's right is real and silent until tested and verified.
heading: The Machine That Writes The Code

text: First, correctness isn't guaranteed. Language models predict plausible-looking code, not provably correct code. The output can be syntactically valid Python, run without errors on simple inputs, and still contain a logic bug that surfaces only in edge cases. The only real defense is to run it, not just read it, which is why using AI to develop code has made testing and verification matter more than ever. Second, instruction-following varies dramatically by model. We asked for "only code, no explanation," and DeepSeek-Coder completely ignored that. If you're building a pipeline that processes model output programmatically, the model's ability to follow format constraints and instructions matters as much as its ability to write good code. Third, all of these models were trained on public code, which means they've absorbed the mistakes, anti-patterns, and security vulnerabilities present in public repositories alongside the good code. A model that frequently saw open(path) without error handling will also cheerfully suggest open(path) without error handling. Finally, none of these models know what your specific codebase does, what your tests cover, or what your teammates expect. They're pattern-matching engines with no understanding of your specific context. However, augmentation of models (RAG) helps a lot, and it's the topic of the next chapter.
heading: Limitations of LLM-Based Code Generation

text: 1. Spring the operator-flip trap yourself. Use codesimilarity on two functions that differ by a single operator, like one returning age >= 21 and one returning age < 21, and watch how high the score stays even though the functions are logical opposites. Then write a sentence explaining why this means a similarity score can never stand in for a correctness check. 2. Build a small semantic code search. Collect five or six short functions of your own, then run codesearch with a plain-English query that shares no words with any of the function names. Check whether the top result is the one you had in mind, and see if you can craft a query that confidently returns the wrong function. 3. Put the local coding models head to head. Pick a simple task, like writing isprime or reversing a string, and ask two or three of them (for example qwen2.5-coder, codellama, and deepseek-coder) to write it. Run each answer against a handful of your own test cases, including a couple of tricky edge cases, and find an example that looks perfectly reasonable but fails your tests. 4. Sort syntax from semantics on paper.
heading: Exercises

text: For each of these edits, decide before running anything whether it changes what the code means: renaming every variable, flipping >= to <, swapping the order of two lines that don't depend on each other, and changing a loop's end condition by one. Then write a line on which of these an embedding would notice, and which it would sail past, based on what this chapter measured. 5. Take one of your functions and dress it in misleading clothes: give it a name and comments that describe the opposite of what it does. Then run codesearch with a query matching the comments and one matching the actual logic, and see which one finds it. You're measuring how much of "understanding code" still amounts to reading the labels. 6. Pick a small real task from your own work, and before asking any model for code, write five test cases for it. Then have two local coding models write the function and run your tests against both. Score them only on tests passed and compare.
heading: Exercises

text: Further Reading UniXcoder is a unified cross-modal code embedding model from Microsoft Research trained across multiple programming languages. HumanEval is the standard benchmark for evaluating code generation models. Program synthesis with large language models, including fill-in-the-middle training, is explored in . GitHub Copilot put a code-writing model inside the editors of more than a million developers , with controlled studies reporting tasks completed substantially faster with its help .
heading: Exercises

text: Abstract Retrieval-Augmented Generation, or RAG, gives a language model access to knowledge it was never trained on. We build a working system one piece at a time: chunking documents, embedding and indexing the chunks in a real vector database, retrieving the right ones through dense, sparse, and hybrid search, then grounding the answer in cited sources. Our knowledge base is one no model has ever trained on: this book itself. The final section assembles the whole pipeline into Sophia, the book's own intelligent assistant.

Language models are frozen in time at the moment their training data was collected, and they have no access to our documents, databases, or anything published after their cutoff date. Instead of relying solely on knowledge baked into the model's weights, a RAG system fetches relevant passages from an external source and hands them to the model as context . The model generates a response grounded in that retrieved material and can cite its sources, so the end user can verify every claim. The RAG pipeline has three phases. Indexing processes your documents into a searchable form. Retrieval finds the most relevant passages for a given query. Generation produces a cited answer from those passages. We'll build each phase piece by piece, and our running corpus will be a document we know better than any other: this book. The book you're "holding" isn't in any model's training data, which makes it the perfect dataset. Every correct answer about it has to come through retrieval, because there's nowhere else a model could possibly have learned it (at least in the immediate near future).
heading: The Knowledge Problem

text: "Likely covers". It's a decent guess, really. The model has never seen this book. The right answer here is probably "I have no idea." Instead, the model does what it does best and produces the most plausible continuation: it reads the word augmentation and reaches for the meaning it knows best from its training, data augmentation, the practice of improving a training set with flipped images and paraphrased sentences. However, this chapter is really about dynamic post-training retrieval-based augmentation.
heading: The Knowledge Problem

text: The corpus is this book, loaded from a snapshot in which every paragraph of every chapter has been cleaned and tagged with the chapter and section it came from. To keep our first demo quick we start with a single chapter's worth of passages, the Prompting chapter. The DocumentStore behind the scenes handles embedding, indexing, and retrieval. The rag() function grabs the top matches and passes them to the model. The answers are specific and the facts are sourced. The model doesn't have to guess what this book says.
heading: Our Dataset

text: Each passage is a paragraph of the book's actual prose, carrying its chapter and section as metadata. We load them from a committed snapshot rather than the live chapter files, so the demos in this chapter stay reproducible even while the book itself keeps being edited. With fifty-five passages from the Prompting chapter in hand, we can populate a document store and fire off our first natural-language query, one the chapter we sampled happens to answer well.
heading: Our Dataset

text: That demo store held a single chapter. For the rest of the chapter we'll retrieve from the whole book, so we load the whole book's passages, every chapter and the preface, into one DocumentStore, each tagged with the chapter and section it came from. The first few entries are printed so we can verify the metadata looks right.
heading: Our Dataset

text: Real documents are far too long to embed as single units. A 50-page technical report can't be packed into one vector and searched effectively, because the embedding averages over every idea in the document. Relevant information gets diluted by irrelevant sections. We need to break documents into smaller chunks, each representing a coherent unit of meaning. Chunk too small and we lose the context a sentence needs to make sense. Chunk too large and the embedding becomes diluted. This is one of those parameters where there's no universally correct answer, only tradeoffs. There are two main approaches, from simplest to most principled.
heading: Chunking

text: Q: If modern models have huge context windows, why bother chunking at all? Once we hear "Gemini has a one-million token context window" , the temptation is to skip chunking entirely and dump the whole document into the prompt. There are two good reasons not to. The first is retrieval quality. When we embed an entire fifty-page document as one vector, we're averaging together fifty pages of meaning into a single point in space. That point is the centroid of everything in the document, which means it's specifically about nothing. Small, focused chunks give sharper embeddings, and sharper embeddings give sharper retrieval. The second reason is the so-called lost in the middle effect. Even when a long document does fit, models have measurably worse recall for facts buried in the middle of a long context than for facts at the beginning or the end . So even if we got the whole document into the prompt, the model may not find what we need. We'll run that failure live just below, against a whole zoo of models, and only one of them will hold on to the middle. So the right mental model in practice is: large context windows are great for assembled, curated context. They aren't a replacement for retrieval. They're a place where retrieval gets to be more generous.
heading: Chunking

text: That last claim is easy to measure. We planted a single fact inside about eight thousand tokens of filler and asked each of seven models to find it, once when it sat near the edges and once when it was buried in the middle. At the edges the models recall the fact some of the time; buried in the middle, six of the seven find it 0 percent of the time. Only llama3.1, with its unusually large context window, holds the middle at all, and even it only half the time. Stuffing everything we have into the context isn't just expensive, it hides the things in the middle. The fix is to feed the model only what the current question needs rather than everything we have, which is what this chapter sets out to do.
heading: Chunking

text: How big is the book, in the only unit a model cares about? Take all of it, every passage of prose, every code cell, every line of output, and count it against the window it would need to fit inside.
heading: Chunking

text: Measured with gemma4's own tokenizer, the one Sophia runs on, the book comes to 108,050 tokens, about 82 percent of the context window. The GPT-4 and GPT-4o tokenizers count it a little lower, around 78 percent, which is the other half of the lesson from the Tokens chapter: the count depends on who's counting. So the whole book now fits inside a modern window, but only just, with about a sixth of it left for the question, the retrieved context, and the answer. And that roomy window is a luxury: Ollama's default budget is 4,096 tokens, about 4 percent of the book. Dropping the whole book into one prompt was never practical, so retrieval reads only the passages each question needs.
heading: Chunking

text: To watch the chunkers work we need one document long enough to need chunking. Here's a deliberately tricky one: two excerpts from this book, run together into a single block, the way a careless text export glues sections together. The first half is the Prompting chapter explaining the sampler; the second half is the Multimodal chapter explaining how a vision model reads an image. Keep that seam in mind, because one of our chunkers is about to find it on its own.
heading: Chunking

text: The most principled approach: split the document where the topic shifts. We embed each sentence and compare neighbors. On real prose the similarities are noisier than we might expect, neighboring sentences hover around the middle of the scale even when they belong together, so rather than using a fixed cutoff we split at the deepest dip. This produces more coherent chunks at the cost of more embedding calls upfront, a worthwhile trade for documents where topics change unexpectedly.
heading: Chunking

text: Look at where the split landed. The deepest dip in similarity falls right at the seam where our stitched document switches from the sampler to vision models, the boundary we knew was there and the chunker had to discover. The similarity scores are computed with cosine similarity, the same closeness measure we took apart in the Semantics chapter. The next cell uses our chunk helper to run the same text through five different window sizes and plots the retrieval quality at each.
heading: Chunking

text: With chunks embedded and stored, we can retrieve. The question is how. There are two fundamentally different approaches, and a third that combines them. Dense retrieval compares meaning using embedding vectors. A reader who complains that "the model forgets what I told it earlier in a long chat" will land on the book's passages about the context window even though their words never name it. Dense retrieval generalizes to paraphrase . Sparse retrieval compares exact words using term-frequency statistics. The most common algorithm for this is called BM25 (short for Best Match 25, a label from the series of probabilistic ranking functions Robertson and colleagues developed, with BM25 itself emerging in the 1990s) . Think of BM25 as a very smart version of a search engine from before Google: it counts how often the search words appear in a document, adjusts for document length, and scores matches accordingly. A query for "BM25" itself will score this very paragraph highly because the exact token appears in it. Sparse retrieval excels when the query and document share specific terminology: algorithm names, function names, error codes, version numbers. Dense-only retrieval fails when users type exact error codes: the codes don't appear in the embedding model's training data, so they end up in meaningless vector positions. Keyword search finds them instantly. Hybrid systems handle both cases.
heading: Retrieval

text: Dense retrieval shines when the user's wording differs from the book's wording but the meaning is the same: none of these passages uses the word "forgets," yet the top two are squarely about the model's memory and the context window, found by meaning alone. It falls short when the user wants an exact match on a specific term, like an algorithm's name. Sparse retrieval flips that trade-off: it's fast, exact, and completely indifferent to paraphrase.
heading: Retrieval

text: The contrast between the two strategies becomes clearest on an adversarial query: a bare algorithm name with no surrounding prose. Dense retrieval has to guess what BM25 means from context; keyword search finds it immediately because the string appears verbatim in the text. And since our corpus is this book, the passage it finds is the very paragraph above where we introduced the term, the book retrieving its own definitions.
heading: Retrieval

text: The third strategy combines the two. Hybrid retrieval normalizes the dense and sparse scores onto a common scale and blends them with a weight, often called alpha: zero is pure keyword matching, one is pure semantic search, and values in between mix the two before ranking. Terminology-heavy queries lean toward the sparse side, conceptual ones toward the dense side, and 0.5 is a reasonable default to tune from. We'll score all three strategies side by side a little later, once we have a corpus with ground-truth labels to grade them against.
heading: Retrieval

text: Metadata filtering is a different lever. Rather than blending scores, it narrows the field before any similarity is computed: because we recorded each passage's chapter and heading at ingestion time, we can keep only the passages that match a filter, the equivalent of a WHERE clause on the search. It's the cheapest improvement available, since the passages we screen out never get scored at all, and it fixes something embeddings can't. A reader asking how to defend a model against attacks should land in the Responsible chapter, but embeddings match meaning with no sense of where a passage lives, so the unfiltered top hits scatter across whichever chapters share the vocabulary; pinning the search to the chapter whose job is security fixes that.
heading: Retrieval

text: We can now retrieve several different ways, so we need a way to decide which is best. So far we've been eyeballing the retrieved documents and nodding along; to compare strategies rigorously we need to put a number on quality. The two fundamental metrics are precision and recall. Precision and recall in plain English Think of searching for a friend at a crowded party. Precision is: of the people you tap on the shoulder thinking they might be your friend, how many really are? If you tap 10 people and 7 are your friend, precision is 70%. High precision means you aren't bothering too many strangers. Recall is: of all the people at the party who are your friend, how many did you find? If your friend came with 4 people you know and you only found 2 of them, recall is 50%. High recall means you found most of who you were looking for. The tension: tapping everyone on the shoulder gets you recall of 100% but precision near zero. Tapping no one gets precision of 100% (you never made a wrong tap) but recall of 0%. Good retrieval balances both.
heading: Retrieval

text: To compare retrieval strategies rigorously we need something the book corpus can't give us: ground truth. P@k asks what fraction of the top-k results are relevant, which means somebody has to have decided, ahead of time, which documents are relevant to which query. For the book we'd have to hand-label that ourselves. Instead we'll borrow a corpus that comes pre-labeled: Wikipedia article summaries covering topics in machine learning and AI, where every chunk carries the title of the article it came from. A query about diffusion models is answered by the Diffusionmodel article, so relevance is decided by the labels rather than by our own generous eyeballing. The articles have been fetched from the Wikipedia REST API and cached locally so the notebook runs offline. We chunk each article, embed the chunks, and build a separate wikistore. Then we run the same P@k evaluation we just defined, but on a corpus with enough variety that each strategy has to discriminate.
heading: A Second Corpus, with Ground Truth

text: Our DocumentStore has been a faithful teaching tool, and it has three problems that disqualify it from real work. It re-embeds every passage from scratch each time this notebook runs, 471 embedding calls to rebuild a store we already built yesterday. It keeps its vectors in a Python list and compares the query against every single one, a linear scan that's fine at hundreds of documents and hopeless at millions. And it forgets everything the moment the process exits. A vector database solves all three. We'll use Chroma, an open-source database that runs locally with no server and no account, which makes it the friendliest first step out of toy storage (uv add chromadb and you have it). Chroma persists embeddings to disk, so the corpus is embedded once and reopened instantly forever after. It indexes vectors with HNSW, an approximate-nearest-neighbor structure that finds close matches without comparing against every document. And it stores metadata alongside each vector, with the same filter-then-search move we just built by hand. The BookIndex class in our genai.book module wraps a persistent Chroma collection around the book corpus.
heading: A Real Vector Database

text: The build step checks which passages are already in the collection and embeds only the missing ones, so the first run of this cell pays for 471 embeddings and every later run pays for zero, the index is sitting on disk waiting. That asymmetry is the point of a real database: indexing happens once, querying happens forever. The vectors it stores are the same nomic embeddings our DocumentStore computes, so nothing about retrieval quality changes.
heading: A Real Vector Database

text: One query, three upgrades visible at once: the search went through the HNSW index rather than a linear scan, the chapter argument became a metadata where-filter inside the database rather than a Python loop, and none of the passages were embedded on this run, they were read off disk. The scores are the same cosine similarities we've used all chapter. From here on, when we need the whole book, we'll reach for this index; the DocumentStore stays useful in the demos where we want to see inside the machinery. With storage settled, one problem remains that better indexing can't fix: what happens when the answer simply isn't in the corpus at all.
heading: A Real Vector Database

text: Every technique so far has assumed the answer is somewhere in our corpus, waiting to be surfaced. When it isn't, a plain RAG pipeline doesn't notice: it hands the model whatever the retriever dredged up, and the model, ever eager to please, writes a confident answer anyway. Ask about the 2024 Nobel Prize and get back a fluent paragraph stitched together from an unrelated article about PyTorch. It's the retrieval version of a student who skipped the reading and bluffs through the exam, and it's the failure that makes people stop trusting these systems. Corrective RAG, or CRAG, adds the instinct plain RAG is missing: a gut-check on the retrieved documents before we trust them . A lightweight retrieval evaluator grades the chunks for how well they fit the question, and the grade routes us down one of three paths. If the docs look good (CORRECT), we refine them, keeping the on-topic pieces and dropping any filler, and answer from the corpus as usual. If they look bad (INCORRECT), we throw them out and fall back to a web search for fresh knowledge. If the grade lands in the murky middle (AMBIGUOUS), we hedge and do both.
heading: Corrective RAG

text: The spine of it is short: One substitution from the paper: it trains a small dedicated model to be the evaluator, while we reuse the embeddings we already have, scoring relevance by how close the question sits to its single best retrieved chunk. That's deterministic and costs us nothing extra, but it's a rougher judge than a trained one, and we'll watch it misjudge before we're done. The websearch step is no toy, either: it calls the live internet, a different kind of commitment than reading a file off our own disk, and we'll look hard at what it costs us when we meet the helper. Let's watch the corrective path fire first, on a question our corpus can't possibly answer.
heading: Corrective RAG

text: The showcrag helper runs that whole pipeline on one question and prints what happens at each stage. We asked who won the 2024 Nobel Prize in Physics, a fact that lives nowhere in our machine-learning corpus. RETRIEVE returns the nearest chunk it can find anyway, a snippet from the PyTorch article, and graderetrieval scores it at 0.56, low enough to trip the INCORRECT verdict, so CRAG skips the local answer and reaches for the web. The WEB line shows the exact query string that left the machine, and beneath it the snippet CRAG leaned on: nobelprize.org, plainly stating that the 2024 prize went to John J. Hopfield and Geoffrey Hinton. With that fact in front of it, the ANSWER comes back correct and cited. The same question through plain RAG would have produced a confident essay about neural networks that never mentioned either name. That web step is the one place in this whole book where our code reaches outside the machine, so here's the entire helper: It leans on ddgs, which searches DuckDuckGo with no API key or account, so anyone can run this line. But a live web call behaves nothing like reading a local file. It's nondeterministic: the web changes, so next week's results may differ, which is why the output above is a frozen snapshot.
heading: Corrective RAG

text: It's slow and costly: a search takes a second or more, a tight loop gets throttled, and a paid API charges real money. And it carries a privacy cost people forget: the query string leaves your machine for someone else's server, so a question carrying a user's name or private data has just been handed to a third party. The try/except is the seatbelt: if the network is down, websearch returns an empty list instead of crashing, and the run degrades to "no web help available."
heading: Corrective RAG

text: Now the other branch, on a question our corpus can answer: whether BERT is an encoder or a decoder. RETRIEVE pulls a chunk straight from the BERT article, graderetrieval scores it at 0.77, comfortably over the line, and the verdict is CORRECT, so CRAG never touches the web. REFINE looks over the three retrieved chunks, finds all three on topic, and keeps them; when a stray off-topic chunk slips into the top results, this is the step that drops it. The ANSWER comes back from the corpus alone, correctly calling BERT an encoder, and not a single query ever left the machine. Same pipeline, same evaluator, but the grade decides everything: a good grade keeps us home and cheap, a bad grade sends us to the web. The third route, AMBIGUOUS, is the hedge we haven't shown: when a question scores in the uncertain band, neither clearly answerable nor clearly hopeless, CRAG refines whatever local docs it has and runs the web search, then lets the model answer from both, a little slower and a little more expensive, saved for the cases where guessing wrong would cost the most. Let's measure how often any of this helps.
heading: Corrective RAG

text: We ran five curated questions through both pipelines, five tries each, and graded every answer the deterministic way, by checking whether the known correct fact appears in it, so no model is left to grade itself. On the three questions our corpus can answer, CNN, BERT, and word embeddings, both pipelines score a perfect five out of five: the evaluator graded all three CORRECT, so CRAG kept the local docs, skipped the web, and matched the baseline. That's the result we most needed to see: correction does no harm where none is needed. On the two traps, the Nobel Prize and the 2024 Olympics, plain RAG scores a flat zero, because the corpus has nothing and the model either says so or invents; CRAG scores a perfect five, because the bad grade sent it to the web and the web had the answer. The entire gap between the bars is the trap queries, and that gap is the whole reason CRAG exists. Our evaluator is a rough judge that can misjudge in both directions. A false INCORRECT wastes a web call on a question the corpus could have answered, costing latency but not correctness.
heading: Corrective RAG

text: A false CORRECT is the dangerous one: ask "how does a neural network learn from its errors" and the similarity score comes back at 0.75, a confident CORRECT, even though the retrieved chunks never explain the mechanism, so CRAG trusts them and misses the answer just as standard RAG would. A trained evaluator catches more of these; none catches all. The web is no oracle either: stale pages, marketing copy, the occasional confident falsehood, and CRAG grounds its answer in whatever it finds. CRAG doesn't make the model smarter or the web trustworthy. It buys one specific, valuable thing: the instinct to notice when retrieval has failed and to go looking elsewhere instead of bluffing. That instinct is most of what separates a classroom demo from a system you'd let a stranger rely on. With our retrieved knowledge as good as we can make it, the next question is how the model should write with it, and how to make it show its sources so a reader can check the work.
heading: Corrective RAG

text: Retrieving good context is half the job. The other half is making sure the model stays inside that context rather than drifting into its training memory. A model asked a question with plenty of context will sometimes still reach past it and hallucinate, especially when the context doesn't quite address the question. Good prompt construction makes this much harder to do. A RAG system that doesn't cite its sources is harder to trust. The user has no way to verify whether the answer is grounded in real documents or whether the model wandered. Citations fix that: a reader who sees bracketed sources in an answer can go and check them, and a checkable answer is a trustworthy one.
heading: Grounded Generation and Citation

text: The grounded RAG function ties the whole pipeline together: retrieve, then prompt the model with an explicit instruction to answer only from the provided context. That final instruction is the guardrail that keeps the model from reaching past the book and into its training memory. Let's ask a question whose answer is spread across two passages and watch the citations land.
heading: Grounded Generation and Citation

text: The answer reads naturally, but we shouldn't take it on faith. A hallucination check asks the model to compare the answer against the retrieved documents and flag any claim that isn't supported. More advanced systems fold that judgment into generation itself, training the model to decide when to retrieve and to critique its own use of the evidence . It isn't foolproof, the model can hallucinate in the checker too, but it catches the most obvious fabrications.
heading: Grounded Generation and Citation

text: The checker looks for three outcomes: the answer is fully supported, partially supported, or contains unsupported claims. Let's run it on a precise factual claim, a specific number the book reports from OpenAI's Codex team, where any drift from the source would be easy to spot.
heading: Grounded Generation and Citation

text: The hallucination check we just built asked the model one blunt question about its whole answer: is anything in here unsupported? But it leans on the same model, reading the same answer, in the same breath that produced it. Asking a model to reread its own paragraph and find the lie is like proofreading your own essay at two in the morning: you read what you meant to write, nod along, and sail straight past the typo. Luckily, there's a more careful way. Chain-of-verification, or CoVe, splits self-checking into four small steps . The model writes a quick draft answer, the confident kind that often slips in one wrong detail; turns each claim in that draft into its own narrow yes/no question; answers each little question on its own; and rewrites the answer, keeping only the claims that survived their fact-check. The whole loop is about four lines: The crucial move is in step three. When verifyclaim asks its little question, it doesn't show the model the draft. We never say "you just listed Morocco, are you sure?" We ask, cold, "does Portugal share a land border with Morocco?" and nothing else.
heading: Chain-of-Verification

text: Stripped of the draft, the model isn't defending a guess it already committed to; it's answering one small fact, and a narrow question asked in isolation is far easier to get right than a sprawling one. The paper calls this the factored variant, and the word matters: a checker that can see the draft tends to nod along with it. CoVe uses no retrieval at all; the model interrogates itself. That makes it a complement to everything else in this chapter, not a substitute: it can only catch the mistakes the model is able to notice on its own under a focused question. For a fact the model doesn't have, asking more carefully changes nothing, and that gap is what retrieval is for. Let's watch it work first, on a question that makes our model slip.
heading: Chain-of-Verification

text: The showcove helper runs that same four-step cove loop and prints what happens at each stage. The DRAFT answer to which countries border Portugal comes back as Spain and Morocco, and the second name is the slip: Morocco sits across the water with no shared border at all, so the draft scores 50 percent, one right and one wrong. Then the verification, with each country asked about on its own. Spain comes back yes, with a true reason, so we keep it. Morocco comes back no, the model readily admitting Portugal doesn't border it once the question is put plainly, so we drop it. The REVISED answer keeps only Spain and scores 100 percent. Nothing here needed a single retrieved document; the model knew all along that Portugal doesn't touch Morocco, it just had to be asked the right small question to catch what it had said.
heading: Chain-of-Verification

text: One example is a lucky anecdote, so we ran the same loop over three questions, twelve drafts each, grading every answer against a fixed list of the right countries so that no model is left to judge itself. Precision, on the left, is the share of listed items that are correct; recall, on the right, is the share of the truly correct items we kept. The precision panel is a tidy win. On Portugal the draft over-reaches often enough to sit at 75 percent, and the factored check lifts it to a clean 100. On the states bordering Mexico the draft is nearly always right already, so there's little to fix and CoVe nudges it from 90 to 100. On neither does recall budge: the check keeps every correct answer and trims only the junk. So far this looks like a free lunch. Then there's Brazil. Its draft was already perfectly precise, so precision has nowhere left to climb, but recall falls off a cliff, from 62 percent down to 20. CoVe didn't trim junk here; it threw away correct answers. To see how, we only need to watch a single run.
heading: Chain-of-Verification

text: This is the same loop, on the same kind of question, going badly wrong. The DRAFT is excellent: seven true neighbors of Brazil, every one correct. Then the factored check, the very step that rescued us on Portugal, insists, country by country, that Brazil doesn't border Colombia, or Venezuela, or Guyana, or Suriname, or Bolivia. Every denial is false, and each is delivered with the same calm confidence as the truth. Our keep-only-what-checks-out rule does as it's told and discards five correct countries, leaving a neat, confident, badly incomplete answer. It's the same lesson Reflexion and self-consistency taught us in earlier chapters: a self-check is only ever as good as the checker. When the model holds a stable wrong belief, the isolated question doesn't dislodge it; it hands the model a fresh chance to repeat the mistake. No amount of careful self-questioning can teach a model a fact it doesn't have. That isn't a knock on the method, it's the line where self-verification stops and retrieval has to take over, which is why this chapter exists. Hand the model Brazil's real border list from a trusted source and the question never comes up.
heading: Chain-of-Verification

text: CoVe isn't free, either: a draft plus one question per listed item can turn one model call into five or ten, so we spend it where a wrong detail is expensive, not on every answer we generate. And it works with grounding, not against it: let retrieval put real facts in front of the model, and let a verification pass catch the careless slips that sneak in anyway. So far every question we've asked has stood on its own; the last piece of a real retrieval system is the conversation, where each new question leans on the ones that came before.
heading: Chain-of-Verification

text: Every mechanism in this chapter exists to solve a specific problem. Alone, each is a useful technique. Assembled, they form a production-grade retrieval system, and ours deserves a name. Meet Sophia, this book's own assistant: a reader's companion whose entire knowledge base is the corpus we just built. She screens incoming questions for injection attempts, rewrites follow-ups into standalone queries, retrieves from the persistent Chroma index with metadata in hand, and answers only from the book, with numbered citations. The code is in genai.book, and nearly every line of it traces back to a concept introduced above. Two of its steps are new here. The first rewrites an elliptical follow-up into a standalone query before searching, so the thread of the conversation survives from one turn to the next. The second is that injection screen, which guards a problem we haven't faced yet: everything a retrieval system ingests or retrieves is untrusted input, and a scraped page or an uploaded document can carry instructions that read as if they came from us. Sophia checks each incoming question for override phrases before it touches the retrieval layer, and the Responsible chapter treats this attack surface, prompt injection, in full. Let's give her a conversation with all three kinds of question: a plain one, an elliptical follow-up, and an attack.
heading: Putting It All Together

text: Three turns, three layers visible. The first answer is grounded and cited. The second question never says the word temperature, and the follow-up rewriting recovers it from the earlier turn before retrieving. The third is the classic override attempt, and it bounces off the injection screen before any retrieval happens at all. Sophia folds all of it into a single object the calling code never needs to think about.
heading: Putting It All Together

text: We started with a model writing a confident book report on a book it had never opened, and we spent the chapter teaching it to read before answering. That's all RAG is. We chunked, embedded, and indexed the book's own chapters, pulled the right passages back with hybrid search, and wrapped everything in grounded generation that cites its sources, growing a ten-line demo into Sophia, the book's own assistant. The idea to carry forward is that retrieval changes what the model is reading, not who the model is, which is why a grounded answer with citations is so much easier to trust. So far, though, Sophia only answers questions. The Agentic chapter gives the model the ability to act, and retrieval becomes one tool among many it can reach for on its own.
heading: Conclusion

text: 1. Feel the chunking trade-off for yourself. Take a long document (the chapter's LONGDOC works, or paste in something of your own), run chunk at a small size like 10 and a large size like 80, then embed a question and find the best-matching chunk at each size. Notice how chunks that are too large dilute the match while chunks that are too small lose the context a sentence needs to make sense. 2. Build a small DocumentStore and query it two different ways: once with a conceptual paraphrase that shares no words with the passage you want, and once with an exact token like a function name or an algorithm's name. See where dense retrieval wins and where plain keyword matching wins, and you'll understand in your bones why hybrid search wants both. 3. Make a grounded system hallucinate, then catch it. Ask groundedrag or Sophia a question whose answer isn't in this book, and watch whether the system admits the gap or invents something. Then write a sentence on why grounding reduces hallucination without ever fully eliminating it. 4. Diagnose the pipeline without touching it.
heading: Exercises

text: For each failure, name the guilty stage and the fix this chapter taught: the answer was never in the corpus; the answer exists but is split across two chunks; the user's words share nothing with the document's words; two retrieved passages flatly contradict each other. One failure per line, stage plus remedy, no code required. 5. Compute precision and recall by hand. Write six short documents, decide yourself which are relevant to one query, then retrieve the top three with your DocumentStore. Count what fraction of the three you fetched was relevant (precision) and what fraction of all the relevant ones you found (recall). Now retrieve five instead of three and watch one number rise while the other falls. That tension is the trade-off, and you just measured it with arithmetic you could do in your head. 6. Build "ask my syllabus". Load a real document you live by, a course syllabus, a lab manual, a rulebook, into a DocumentStore, wire it to groundedrag, and ask it five questions you care about, including one whose answer you know isn't in there. Report the hits, the misses, and what the system did on the unanswerable one. Then run one answer through the chain-of-verification loop and note whether any claim got revised.
heading: Exercises

text: Further Reading The original RAG paper is , which formalized the pipeline this chapter implements, and surveys how far the field has run with the idea since. Dense passage retrieval, which underpins modern vector search, was established in , while the classic keyword baseline it improves on is the BM25 probabilistic model . ColBERT's late-interaction architecture lands between the two, and Fusion-in-Decoder shows how a generator grows stronger as it's fed more retrieved passages. Scaling retrieval to trillions of tokens is explored in . Self-RAG folds retrieval and self-critique into the model's own decoding . A lighter-weight cousin, Chain-of-Note, has the model jot a brief note on each retrieved passage before it answers, so an irrelevant hit is less likely to drag the response off course . For measuring whether any of it works, proposes automated RAG-specific metrics, and documents the lost-in-the-middle effect that makes tight retrieval worth the trouble. The security aspects of retrieval injection are an active area; provides early grounding in adversarial attacks on NLP systems.
heading: Exercises

text: Abstract Turning talkers into doers is the transition known as agentic AI. We build up the core capabilities one at a time: tool use and function calling, recovery from failure, memory across turns, and self-correction. Rather than crown one model and trust it, we treat every capability as an audition: a handful of local models compete, the winner is read straight off a chart, and it steps into the role of Sophia, this book's own assistant, who learned to read in the Augmentation chapter and here learns to act. The surprises are the point, because the model that wins one skill routinely loses the next.

A chatbot answers a question and waits for the next one. An agent is handed a goal and goes after it, taking one action after another until the goal is met or it decides the goal can't be reached. We can model this as a loop: the agent reads its goal, decides on an action, takes that action in some environment, observes what changed, updates its picture of the world, and decides again. We can interleave a reasoning step with an acting step so the model can think, do, look, and think again . Almost every agent built since is a variation on that one loop .
heading: From Talker to Doer

text: The loop sounds simple, and for one or two turns it is. The trouble starts when the goal needs thirty turns, because each step depends on the outcome of the last and a single wrong move early on silently poisons everything after it. The agent doesn't stop to report a potentially catastrophic error or omission; it moves on to the next task on its list. The field has a name for this difficulty, long-horizon reasoning, and it's the gap that separates a model that writes a fluent paragraph from a system that can carry out a plan from Monday to Sunday without losing the plot. In just a few years we went from a model that could reason and act in a loop, to models that taught themselves to call tools, to agents that fix real bugs in real codebases and drive our computers by looking at our screens.
heading: From Talker to Doer

text: Three of those landmarks matter most for what follows. ReAct gave us the reasoning-and-acting loop this chapter is built on . Voyager set an agent loose in Minecraft to build a growing library of its own skills , a move we'll borrow in the Autonomy chapter. And Anthropic shipped a model that operates a computer the way a person does , the frontier the rest of the book reaches toward.
heading: From Talker to Doer

text: An agent that always runs the exact same plan will keep running it long after it stops working, like a GPS insisting you turn onto a road that's been closed for months. An agent that explores too freely never settles on an answer at all. Good design calibrates between the two on the fly, exploiting a path that works and exploring only when it gets stuck. The scaffolding that runs the loop, calls the model, routes each tool call to the right handler, and decides when to stop, is the agent runtime, and in this chapter that runtime is the genai.agent module. We'll call into it rather than reprint it.
heading: From Talker to Doer

text: A model that only emits text can't make real change in the world. We'll need tools to bridge from language to action; they're what turn a chatbot into something far more useful. We just need to describe each tool to the model: its function name, a sentence of description, and a list of parameters.
heading: Giving Our Agent Hands

text: That display comes from showtools(), which prints each tool the way the model itself receives it: a function signature naming the parameters and their types, with the one-sentence description sitting right underneath. Reading down the transcript, calculate(expr: string) takes a single string and is billed as the thing to reach for on any arithmetic, lookup(term: string) takes a term and promises a definition from this book's glossary, and remember(text: string) takes a note and files it away for the reader. Each of those three was built by toolspec, the small helper that packs a name, a description, and a typed parameter list into the JSON schema the model expects.
heading: Giving Our Agent Hands

text: Not every model can call tools, and on Ollama the giveaway is a small tools tag on the model's profile page . Calling a tool is a learned skill, not a built-in one: a model has to be trained to stop mid-sentence and emit a structured request, naming a function and its arguments in a format a program can parse . A model that's wonderful at conversation but never saw a tool call in training doesn't know these conventions.
heading: Giving Our Agent Hands

text: Sophia left the Augmentation chapter with one ability: handed a question, she could search this book and answer from it. Useful, but everything she might do about the answer, file a note, work a calculation, check a definition against the glossary, was still on the reader. Across this chapter we give her hands. Sophia is a role, not a model, and we hold a fresh audition for every capability the job needs. The models that lose aren't wasted; they're the contrast that shows us what winning even looks like. The first skill is the one every other capability is built on: reaching for the right tool, with the right arguments, at the right moment. We score seven models on eight prompts a reader might send. Two are gentle one-tool requests, and two are word problems where the model has to build the arithmetic itself instead of copying a number off the page. Two more need a pair of tool calls chained in a single turn, and the last two are pure small talk where the right move is to reach for no tool at all. That final pair tells a deliberate caller apart from an eager one. Each prompt earns partial credit on two questions: did the model pick the right tool, and did it pass the right arguments?
heading: An Audition

text: The eight tool-calling prompts. Two need a single tool, two hide the arithmetic in a word problem, two chain a pair of tools in one turn, and the last two should trigger no tool at all, which is what tells a deliberate caller from an eager one.
heading: An Audition

text: gemma4 runs the table alone, the only model to ace all eight prompts, and at about two and a third seconds a call it's among the quickest of the capable models too, so it takes the role without a tiebreak and will play Sophia by default for the rest of the chapter. qwen3 is close behind at 94%, stumbling only once. llama3.1 lands third at 75%, easy to overlook, but remember the name, because the auditions ahead, in this chapter and the next, keep finding jobs where careful beats brilliant. The bottom half of the board is where the real lesson sits. functiongemma is a tiny function-tuned model, several times faster than anything else at half a second a call, but it's also among the weakest at 50%. When we get to routing in the Autonomy chapter we'll watch it refuse anything that isn't a function call, which makes it a poor general worker. And then there's mistral at the bottom with 37.5%. Its failing isn't that it calls tools badly; mostly it doesn't call them at all, preferring to answer from its own memory. That works out fine when it happens to know that 144 divided by 12 is 12, but it means mistral would cheerfully make up a definition rather than look up the one this book uses.
heading: An Audition

text: A fluent talker isn't automatically a capable agent, and an audition is how we tell the two apart. With the part cast, here's gemma4 as Sophia, handed a single run-on request and reaching for three different tools to answer it: a calculator, a glossary lookup, and a note-keeper.
heading: An Audition

text: asksophia() is the helper doing the talking here. It runs the prompt through runwithtrace, the same ReAct loop as runagent but one that also hands back the list of tool calls Sophia made, and then prints the whole exchange as a transcript. Read it top to bottom. The USER line is the reader's run-on request, three chores crammed into one sentence. The first TOOL line shows Sophia building the arithmetic herself, calculate(expr=(600 - 250) /...) returning 14.0, the days left at the reader's pace. The second TOOL line is lookup(term=attention) pulling the definition straight from this book's own glossary, the same one printed in the back matter. The third is remember(text=My book club ...) filing the note and reporting back saved=True. The closing SOPHIA line is the answer she writes once the tools are done, folding all three results into one tidy reply. Three different tools, chosen and called by Sophia herself, all triggered from a single prompt. Nothing in the cell told her which tool to use for which part; she read the tool descriptions and decided.
heading: An Audition

text: We can think of our tool descriptions as the labels on the boxes in a closet: a vague label sends the model rummaging through the wrong box, while a precise label that says exactly what's inside, and when to reach for it, finds the right box almost every time. Research shows that the quality of tool documentation is the difference between a model that calls an API correctly and one that hallucinates the arguments . So let's hold an audition for documentation itself. We put the same six glossary questions to all seven models twice over: once with a precise label on the lookup tool, "Look up a generative AI term in the book's glossary," and once with a uselessly vague one, "Returns a string for the given input." Then we count how often each model still reaches for the glossary. A model that keeps calling under the vague label is reading the question's intent past the bad documentation; one whose call-rate falls off a cliff was leaning on the label to know what the box was even for.
heading: A Carefully Curated Closet

text: The six glossary terms. Each is put to every model twice as "What does 'X' mean in this book?", once with the precise label "Look up a generative AI term in the book's glossary" and once with the useless "Returns a string for the given input."
heading: A Carefully Curated Closet
